tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (neoadjuvant, preferred)

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 2 doses (NADINA: ipilimumab 80 mg + nivolumab 240 mg Q3W; PRADO/OpACIN-neo: ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg Q3W)

trial_data.results: NADINA: 12-month EFS 83.7% vs. 57.2% with adjuvant nivolumab; PRADO: 2-year RFS 79% with MPR rate 61%; OpACIN-neo: 3-year RFS 82%, OS 92%, MPR rate 64%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Neoadjuvant ipilimumab+nivolumab is category 1 only for initial presentation of stage III disease with clinically positive nodes that have not received prior targeted or immune therapy. Ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg is generally preferred due to lower toxicity compared to ipi3/nivo1. The NADINA trial used flat dosing (ipi 80 mg + nivo 240 mg). Major pathologic response (MPR) after 2 doses is associated with >90% 3-year RFS with no additional adjuvant therapy. Optimal adjuvant therapy after neoadjuvant is not clear; anti-PD-1 monotherapy or observation for MPR, or anti-PD-1 or dabrafenib/trametinib for those lacking MPR. This regimen should only be considered for low-volume in-transit disease with caution due to high toxicity risk.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab (neoadjuvant, preferred)

trial_data.results: 2-year EFS 72% vs. 49% for adjuvant pembrolizumab alone; OS data not mature; MPR rate 53%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab followed by surgery then adjuvant pembrolizumab to complete 1 year of therapy. The SWOG1801 trial compared neoadjuvant-adjuvant pembrolizumab vs. adjuvant pembrolizumab alone. Neoadjuvant approach improved EFS. Pathologic response assessment is important; patients with MPR have favorable outcomes. This regimen is an option for resectable stage III–IV melanoma. Note that pembrolizumab is not category 1 in this setting; the category 1 designation is for nivolumab/ipilimumab in clinically positive nodes. The panel notes that two different clinical trials (NADINA and SWOG1801) have compared neoadjuvant regimens vs. adjuvant anti-PD-1, but it is unclear which neoadjuvant regimen is more active since they have not been directly compared.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab (neoadjuvant, other recommended)

trial_data.results: Demonstrated efficacy, but not studied in larger randomized studies with modern comparators or against active adjuvant therapy

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab monotherapy as neoadjuvant has shown activity in smaller studies. It is listed as 'Other Recommended' in the neoadjuvant setting. The optimal dose and schedule are not well established, with 1–3 doses every 2 weeks used in trials. This regimen is less preferred than combination checkpoint blockade but may be considered for patients who cannot tolerate combination therapy. For low-volume in-transit disease, the high risk of toxicities with combination regimens may outweigh benefit, making single-agent nivolumab a reasonable alternative.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab and Relatlimab-rmbw (neoadjuvant, other recommended)

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II study (Amaria et al. Nature 2022)
trial_data.results: MPR rate and outcomes favorable but not compared in larger randomized studies

tier_1_regimens.notes: Neoadjuvant nivolumab/relatlimab has been studied in a phase II trial with 2 doses every 4 weeks. This regimen is listed as 'Other Recommended' due to lack of large randomized data with modern comparators. The optimal adjuvant approach after neoadjuvant nivolumab/relatlimab is not well defined; one study gave adjuvant nivolumab/relatlimab for a total of 1 year, but single-agent anti-PD-1 therapy should be considered. For low-volume in-transit disease, the high risk of toxicities may outweigh benefit.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (neoadjuvant, useful in certain circumstances)

trial_data.results: MPR achieved; however, this approach has not been studied in comparison with adjuvant dabrafenib and trametinib

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dabrafenib/trametinib is considered in the neoadjuvant setting only if immunotherapy is contraindicated. A short course of 4–12 weeks of preoperative therapy can be considered. If BRAF V600 mutation positive, other BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations (e.g., encorafenib/binimetinib, vemurafenib/cobimetinib) can be considered in the event of unacceptable toxicities or based on side effect profiles. This regimen is not preferred over immunotherapy-based neoadjuvant approaches.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab (adjuvant, stage IIB/IIC preferred)

trial_data.trial_name: CheckMate 915? Not specified for stage II; FDA approval based on trials in stage III and extrapolation
trial_data.results: Approved for clinical or pathologic stage IIB/IIC; reduces relapse events; longer follow-up needed for OS impact

tier_1_regimens.notes: Adjuvant nivolumab is approved for pathologic stage IIB/IIC melanoma. Pathologic staging with SLNB is strongly recommended before consideration. Shared decision-making should weigh risk of recurrence (approximately 15% risk of lifelong endocrine dysfunction) vs. benefit. Observation remains an option, especially for lower risk stage IIB. The recommended duration is 12 months based on clinical trials. Nivolumab 240 mg every 2 weeks or 480 mg every 4 weeks are FDA-approved dosing schedules.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab (adjuvant, stage IIB/IIC preferred)

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-716? Not specified; FDA approval based on stage III trials and extrapolation
trial_data.results: Reduces relapse events; longer follow-up needed for OS impact

tier_1_regimens.notes: Adjuvant pembrolizumab is approved for pathologic stage IIB/IIC melanoma. Similar considerations as for nivolumab. Duration 12 months. FDA-approved dosing: 200 mg every 3 weeks or 400 mg every 6 weeks. Shared decision-making strongly recommended due to risk of long-term immunotoxicities.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab (adjuvant, stage III preferred)

trial_data.results: At 1.6 years follow-up: 1-year RFS 71% vs. 61% for ipilimumab; HR 0.65; OS not reported at initial analysis; 4-year outcomes show comparable OS to ipilimumab

tier_1_regimens.notes: Adjuvant nivolumab is recommended for stage III melanoma including sentinel node positive, clinically positive nodes, and clinical satellite/in-transit disease (category 1 for CSIT limited resectable). In CheckMate 238, patients had resected stage IIIB/C or stage IV and were required to have CLND. However, based on MSLT-II and DeCOG trials showing no OS benefit from CLND, CLND should not be a factor in adjuvant therapy decisions for sentinel node-positive patients. Duration of therapy: 12 months. For patients with prior anti-PD-1 exposure, ipilimumab may be considered as an alternative.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab (adjuvant, stage III preferred)

trial_data.results: At 1.2 years follow-up: 1-year RFS 75% vs. 61% for placebo; HR 0.57; OS data not mature; DMFS improved

tier_1_regimens.notes: Adjuvant pembrolizumab is category 1 for stage III melanoma (clinically detected nodal metastases; for clinically occult nodal disease, category 1 is limited to stage IIIA with SLN metastasis >1 mm or stage IIIB/C per AJCC 7th). For satellite/in-transit disease, it is category 2A. Duration 12 months. The NCCN Panel extends the indication to all stage III patients at high risk of recurrence. Subgroup analyses show benefit regardless of PD-L1 expression or BRAF mutation status.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (adjuvant, stage III preferred, BRAF V600+)

trial_data.results: 3-year RFS 58% vs. 39% for placebo; HR 0.47; 3-year OS 86% vs. 77%; HR 0.57 but did not meet prespecified interim boundary (P=0.0006). DMFS improved: HR 0.51

tier_1_regimens.notes: Adjuvant dabrafenib/trametinib is recommended for patients with stage III melanoma and BRAF V600E/K mutations. The COMBI-AD trial required CLND for entry, but as with anti-PD-1, CLND should not be a factor in decision-making. Duration 12 months. If BRAF V600 mutation positive, other BRAF/MEK inhibitor combinations (encorafenib/binimetinib, vemurafenib/cobimetinib) can be considered in case of unacceptable toxicities. This regimen is also preferred for nodal recurrence and resectable local satellite/in-transit recurrence. Note: category 1 for clinically positive nodes and for sentinel node positive with high-risk features; category 2A for satellite/in-transit disease.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab (adjuvant, useful in certain circumstances, after anti-PD-1)

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then every 3 months for up to 3 years (high-dose regimen 10 mg/kg was tested but 3 mg/kg is preferred for safety)

trial_data.results: EORTC 18071: 5-year RFS 41% vs. 30% for placebo; HR 0.76; OS improved (5-year 65% vs. 54%; HR 0.72). ECOG 1609 suggests 3 mg/kg may have similar RFS with lower toxicity

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab is recommended as adjuvant therapy only for patients with prior exposure to anti-PD-1 therapy (e.g., progression during anti-PD-1 with resectable disease). The recommended dose is 3 mg/kg based on better safety profile, although the FDA approval is for 10 mg/kg. The regimen is associated with high toxicity (grade 3–4 AEs 54% with 10 mg/kg, 37% with 3 mg/kg) and 1% drug-related mortality. Due to availability of better-tolerated options (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, dabrafenib/trametinib), ipilimumab is not a preferred adjuvant therapy for first-line.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (adjuvant, for resected stage IV oligometastatic, preferred)

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses (induction), then nivolumab maintenance

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.dose: 1 mg/kg (during induction) then 240 mg Q2W or 480 mg Q4W
drugs.schedule: Induction: 4 doses, then maintenance for up to 12 months

trial_data.results: Ipilimumab 3 mg/kg + nivolumab 1 mg/kg improved RFS compared with nivolumab or ipilimumab alone in resected stage IV melanoma

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with resected oligometastatic stage IV melanoma, adjuvant ipilimumab+nivolumab is a preferred option. The IMMUNED trial showed improved RFS with combination compared to monotherapy. Note that nivolumab monotherapy and pembrolizumab monotherapy are also category 1 options in this setting. Ipilimumab+nivolumab combination has higher toxicity; patient selection is important. Duration of therapy is not well defined; typically 12 months total.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab (adjuvant, for resected stage IV oligometastatic, preferred)

trial_data.results: 1-year RFS 71% vs. 61% for ipilimumab; HR 0.65

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab is FDA approved for adjuvant treatment of melanoma with lymph node involvement or metastatic disease after complete resection. Category 1 for resected stage IV. Duration 12 months. This is a preferred option along with pembrolizumab and ipilimumab+nivolumab. For patients who progress on anti-PD-1 therapy, ipilimumab monotherapy (3 mg/kg) may be considered.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab (adjuvant, for resected stage IV oligometastatic, preferred)

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-054 (did not include stage IV; extrapolation from stage III)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is recommended as adjuvant therapy for resected stage IV disease based on high-level evidence from stage III trials and its known efficacy in metastatic disease. Category 1 recommendation. Duration 12 months. The NCCN Panel has extended the indication to stage IV due to similarity to nivolumab, which has phase III data in this setting.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (adjuvant, for resected stage IV, other recommended, BRAF V600+)

trial_data.trial_name: Extrapolation from COMBI-AD (stage III) and metastatic data

tier_1_regimens.notes: BRAF/MEK targeted therapy is listed as 'Other Recommended' for adjuvant treatment of resected stage IV melanoma (category 2B). The COMBI-AD trial did not include stage IV patients, but extrapolation from metastatic and stage III data supports use. Other BRAF/MEK combinations (encorafenib/binimetinib, vemurafenib/cobimetinib) are also category 2B options. This is less preferred than immune checkpoint inhibitors due to lower level of evidence in this setting.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (first-line metastatic, preferred)

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses (induction), then nivolumab maintenance

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.dose: 1 mg/kg (during induction) then 240 mg Q2W or 480 mg Q4W
drugs.schedule: Induction: 4 doses, then maintenance until progression or unacceptable toxicity

trial_data.results: ORR 58%, median PFS 11.5 months, median OS not reached (4-year OS 53%); compared to nivolumab (ORR 45%, median PFS 6.9 months, 4-year OS 46%) and ipilimumab (ORR 19%, median PFS 2.9 months, 4-year OS 30%). CheckMate 069: ORR 59% vs 11% for ipilimumab.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Combination ipilimumab/nivolumab is a preferred first-line therapy for metastatic or unresectable melanoma, category 1. It is associated with higher response rates and improved PFS and OS compared to anti-PD-1 monotherapy but with higher toxicity (grade 3–4 irAEs ~59%). This regimen has demonstrated intracranial activity and is preferred for patients with asymptomatic brain metastases not requiring corticosteroids. Consider alternative dosing: nivo3/ipi1 (CheckMate 511) has lower toxicity with similar efficacy. Testing for PD-L1 should not guide decision-making. Patients should be monitored closely for immune-related adverse events.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab and Relatlimab-rmbw (first-line metastatic, preferred)

trial_data.results: Median PFS 10.1 vs 4.6 months for nivolumab; HR 0.75; OS improvement also reported

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab + relatlimab is a fixed-dose combination given IV every 4 weeks. Category 1 for first-line therapy. It offers improved PFS over nivolumab alone with intermediate toxicity between anti-PD-1 monotherapy and ipilimumab/nivolumab combination. The RELATIVITY-047 trial established its efficacy. It is also an option for second-line therapy after anti-PD-1 progression (ORR 9–12%). For low-volume in-transit disease, the high toxicity of this combination may outweigh benefit.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab (first-line metastatic, preferred)

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity

trial_data.results: CheckMate 066: ORR 43% vs 14% for DTIC, median OS 37.5 vs 11.2 months. CheckMate 067: ORR 45%, median PFS 6.9 months, median OS 36.9 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab monotherapy is a category 1 first-line option. It has a favorable safety profile compared to combination therapy, with grade 3–4 treatment-related AEs ~22%. It is effective regardless of BRAF mutation status or PD-L1 expression. Durable responses are seen, with median duration not reached in some trials. For patients with asymptomatic brain metastases, combination therapy is preferred due to higher intracranial activity, but nivolumab monotherapy is an option for those not candidates for combination.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab (first-line metastatic, preferred)

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.schedule: Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity

trial_data.results: ORR 37% (Q2W) and 36% (Q3W) vs 13% for ipilimumab; 2-year OS 55% vs 43%; median OS not reached vs 16 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is category 1 for first-line therapy. Similar efficacy and safety to nivolumab. Dosing flexibility: 200 mg Q3W or 400 mg Q6W (FDA approved). Pembrolizumab has shown a more favorable tolerability profile than ipilimumab in KEYNOTE-006. Long-term follow-up shows durable responses and complete responses can occur beyond 12 months. For patients with brain metastases, combination therapy is preferred but pembrolizumab has intracranial activity as shown in trials.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (first-line metastatic, other recommended, BRAF V600+)

drugs.name: Dabrafenib
drugs.schedule: Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity

drugs.name: Trametinib
drugs.schedule: Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity

trial_data.results: COMBI-d: ORR 69%, median PFS 11.0 months, median OS 25.1 months. COMBI-v: ORR 64%, median PFS 11.4 months, median OS not reached vs 17.2 months for vemurafenib

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dabrafenib/trametinib is a category 1 option for first-line therapy in BRAF V600-mutant melanoma. It is listed as 'Other Recommended' because immune checkpoint inhibitors are generally preferred due to OS benefit. However, BRAF/MEK inhibition may be preferred for patients with symptomatic, rapidly progressing disease or high tumor burden who need a quick response. Toxicity includes pyrexia (~55%), chills, fatigue, arthralgia, and cutaneous AEs. Management includes holding therapy at onset of pyrexia; low-dose corticosteroids if prolonged.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Encorafenib/Binimetinib (first-line metastatic, other recommended, BRAF V600+)

drugs.name: Encorafenib
drugs.schedule: Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity

drugs.name: Binimetinib
drugs.schedule: Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity

trial_data.results: Median PFS 14.9 months vs 7.3 months for vemurafenib; adjusted HR 0.56; median OS 33.6 months vs 16.9 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Encorafenib/binimetinib is a category 1 option for BRAF V600-mutant melanoma. It showed superior PFS and OS compared to vemurafenib monotherapy. The COLUMBUS trial demonstrated a favorable safety profile compared to other BRAF/MEK combinations, with lower rates of pyrexia and photosensitivity. However, headache, nausea, and diarrhea are common. Considered an alternative to dabrafenib/trametinib.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Vemurafenib/Cobimetinib (first-line metastatic, other recommended, BRAF V600+)

drugs.name: Vemurafenib
drugs.schedule: Until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity

drugs.name: Cobimetinib
drugs.schedule: 21 days on, 7 days off until progression or toxicity

trial_data.results: ORR 70% vs 50%; median PFS 12.3 vs 7.2 months; median OS 22.3 vs 17.4 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vemurafenib/cobimetinib is category 1 for BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma. Similar efficacy to other BRAF/MEK combinations. Unique toxicity includes photosensitivity, serous retinopathy, and elevated CPK. Regular ophthalmologic monitoring recommended. Cobimetinib is given on an intermittent schedule (21/7). This regimen is an alternative to dabrafenib/trametinib or encorafenib/binimetinib.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Low-dose Ipilimumab + Pembrolizumab (first-line metastatic, other recommended)

trial_data.results: ORR and safety profile acceptable; long-term follow-up reported

tier_1_regimens.notes: Low-dose ipilimumab combined with standard-dose pembrolizumab is a category 2B option for first-line therapy. It may offer an intermediate toxicity profile compared to standard ipilimumab/nivolumab. This regimen is also an option for second-line after anti-PD-1 progression. The evidence level is lower (category 2B) due to lack of phase III data specifically for this combination in melanoma.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (second-line, preferred, after anti-PD-1)

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then nivolumab monotherapy 240 mg Q2W or 480 mg Q4W

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II trial (Olson et al. JCO 2021?) - cited in MELSYS 3: 94-patient trial randomized to ipi/nivo vs ipi after anti-PD-1 progression
trial_data.results: ORR 28% vs 8% for ipilimumab alone; 6-month PFS 35% vs 13%

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients who progress on anti-PD-1 therapy, combination ipilimumab/nivolumab is a preferred second-line option. It has been shown to be superior to ipilimumab alone after anti-PD-1 failure. Category 2A. Toxicity is significant but manageable. For patients who progressed on single-agent anti-PD-1, this combination can produce durable responses in approximately 25–33% of patients.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab and Relatlimab-rmbw (second-line, preferred, after anti-PD-1)

trial_data.results: ORR 9–12% in PD-1/PD-L1 refractory disease

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab/relatlimab is a preferred option for second-line therapy after anti-PD-1 progression. The response rate is modest (9–12%) but durable responses possible. It is also a first-line option (category 1). For second-line, category 2A. Consider for patients who need an alternative to ipilimumab-containing regimens.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Anti-PD-1 monotherapy (second-line, preferred, after anti-CTLA-4 or other)

trial_data.results: CheckMate 037: ORR 27% vs 10% for chemo; median OS 15.7 vs 14.4 months (NS). KEYNOTE-002: ORR 22-28% vs 4%; 2-year OS 36-38% vs 30% (NS for 2 mg/kg)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Anti-PD-1 monotherapy (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) is a preferred second-line option for patients who have not received prior anti-PD-1. After ipilimumab progression, response rates are lower than in first-line but still clinically meaningful. Category 2A. For patients who progress on anti-PD-1, switching to combination therapy (ipi/nivo or nivo/relatlimab) is preferred over retrying monotherapy.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (second-line, preferred, BRAF V600+)

trial_data.trial_name: Various phase I/II trials in previously treated patients
trial_data.results: Response rates ~15% in BRAFi-pretreated; higher in those with longer interval from prior BRAFi

tier_1_regimens.notes: BRAF/MEK inhibition is a preferred second-line option for patients with BRAF V600 mutation who have not previously received BRAF/MEK inhibitors. For those who progressed on prior BRAF-targeted therapy, rechallenge may be considered after a treatment-free interval. Category 2A (note: corrected from category 1 in earlier version; now category 2A for second-line).

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Encorafenib/Binimetinib (second-line, preferred, BRAF V600+)

trial_data.results: Data extrapolated from first-line setting

tier_1_regimens.notes: Encorafenib/binimetinib is listed as a preferred second-line option for BRAF V600-mutant melanoma (category 2A). It can be used in patients who have not previously received BRAF/MEK inhibitors or as an alternative after toxicity to dabrafenib/trametinib.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Vemurafenib/Cobimetinib (second-line, preferred, BRAF V600+)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Vemurafenib/cobimetinib is a preferred second-line option for BRAF V600-mutant melanoma (category 2A). Similar considerations as for other BRAF/MEK combinations. Toxicity includes photosensitivity, serous retinopathy.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Low-dose Ipilimumab + Pembrolizumab (second-line, preferred, after anti-PD-1)

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-029 Part 1B, Olson et al. JCO 2021
trial_data.results: ORR ~25-33% in anti-PD-1 refractory patients; durable responses

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen is specifically recommended for progression following anti-PD-1 therapy. Low-dose ipilimumab plus pembrolizumab may produce response rates of approximately 25–33% after anti-PD-1 failure. Category 2A. It is a preferred option in this setting, alongside ipilimumab/nivolumab and nivolumab/relatlimab.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Lifileucel (TIL therapy, second-line, preferred, after anti-PD-1 and BRAF/MEK if applicable)

drugs.name: Lifileucel (tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes)
drugs.schedule: Single infusion followed by high-dose IL-2

trial_data.results: Durable response rates in anti-PD-1 refractory melanoma; specific rates not provided in source

tier_1_regimens.notes: TIL therapy with lifileucel is a preferred option for second-line or subsequent therapy in patients with good performance status who have progressed on anti-PD-1 therapy and BRAF/MEK inhibition (if BRAF mutant). It requires a resectable metastasis for TIL harvesting and includes nonmyeloablative chemotherapy and high-dose IL-2. Not recommended for patients with inadequate cardiac, pulmonary, or renal function, poor performance status, or untreated/active brain metastases. Early referral to a center with melanoma expertise is strongly considered after anti-PD-1 progression.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ipilimumab (second-line, other recommended, after anti-PD-1, if combination not feasible)

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses (reinduction can be considered)

trial_data.trial_name: Phase III trials (CA184-002, CA184-024) in ipilimumab-naïve; retreatment data
trial_data.n_patients: Not specified for second-line post anti-PD-1
trial_data.results: Response rates ~10-15% as monotherapy after prior therapy; durable responses possible

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab monotherapy is an option for second-line therapy when combination immunotherapy is not feasible or contraindicated. It is less effective than combination regimens. For patients who previously responded to ipilimumab and then progressed >3 months after discontinuation, reinduction may be considered. Category 2A as 'Other Recommended'.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Trametinib (second-line, useful in certain circumstances, BRAF gene fusion or non-V600 mutation)

biomarkers.marker: BRAF gene fusion or non-V600 BRAF mutation (e.g., L597, K601)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with BRAF gene fusions or non-V600 BRAF mutations (e.g., L597, K601) that are sensitive to MEK inhibition, trametinib may be considered. Evidence is based on case reports and limited clinical data. This is a useful option in certain circumstances when standard therapies are not applicable.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Imatinib (second-line, useful in certain circumstances, KIT mutation positive)

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II trials (Hodi et al., Carvajal et al., Guo et al.)
trial_data.results: ORR 17-30% in KIT-mutated melanoma; higher response in exon 11/13 mutations; minimal activity in KIT amplifications

tier_1_regimens.notes: Imatinib is recommended for patients with KIT mutations (especially exon 11 and 13) in mucosal, acral, or chronic sun-damaged melanoma. Other KIT inhibitors such as nilotinib, dasatinib, or ripretinib can also be considered. KIT amplifications alone have minimal sensitivity. The NCCN guidelines also list nilotinib and dasatinib as options in this category.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Entrectinib (second-line, useful in certain circumstances, NTRK1/2/3 fusion positive)

trial_data.trial_name: Integrated analysis of phase I/II trials
trial_data.results: Activity demonstrated in NTRK fusion-positive tumors; specific melanoma data limited

tier_1_regimens.notes: Entrectinib is an option for NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion-positive melanomas. These fusions are rare (<1% of melanomas). Larotrectinib is also listed as an option. Evidence is from histology-agnostic trials with few melanoma cases.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Larotrectinib (second-line, useful in certain circumstances, NTRK1/2/3 fusion positive)

trial_data.results: ORR ~75% across tumor types; durable responses

tier_1_regimens.notes: Larotrectinib is an alternative to entrectinib for NTRK fusion-positive melanoma. Both are listed as 'Useful in Certain Circumstances' (category 2A). These should be considered when standard therapies are exhausted or inappropriate.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Crizotinib (second-line, useful in certain circumstances, ROS1 fusion positive)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Crizotinib is an option for ROS1 gene fusion-positive melanoma. ROS1 fusions are extremely rare in melanoma. Entrectinib is also listed as an option for ROS1 fusions. Evidence from lung cancer and case reports supports activity.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Entrectinib (second-line, useful in certain circumstances, ROS1 fusion positive)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Entrectinib is another option for ROS1 fusion-positive melanoma. Both crizotinib and entrectinib are listed as 'Useful in Certain Circumstances' (category 2A). These are rarely used due to the low incidence of ROS1 fusions in melanoma.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab (second-line, useful in certain circumstances)

trial_data.results: ORR in anti-PD-1 refractory melanoma; specific rates not provided in the source

tier_1_regimens.notes: Lenvatinib in combination with pembrolizumab is a useful option for patients who have progressed on anti-PD-1 ± ipilimumab and/or nivolumab/relatlimab, and if applicable, BRAF/MEK inhibitors. It is listed under 'Useful in Certain Circumstances' (category 2A). The LEAP-004 trial demonstrated activity in this heavily pretreated population. Also an option for patients not eligible for TIL therapy.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: T-VEC + Ipilimumab (second-line, useful in certain circumstances)

trial_data.results: ORR 39% vs 18%; median PFS 8.2 vs 6.4 months (NS); OS not significantly different (HR 0.8)

tier_1_regimens.notes: The combination of intralesional T-VEC with ipilimumab is a category 2B option for patients with injectable metastases. It showed improved response rate but no significant improvement in PFS or OS compared to ipilimumab alone. This combination is only recommended for second-line or subsequent therapy, not first-line. The toxicities are similar to the individual agents. T-VEC monotherapy is also an option (category 1 for unresectable CSIT).

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib + Pembrolizumab (second-line, useful in certain circumstances, BRAF V600+)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Triplet therapy with BRAF/MEK inhibition plus PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor is listed as a useful option for second-line or subsequent therapy, despite FDA approval in first-line. The NCCN panel recommends this only for second-line due to excessive toxicity with minimal additive benefit when used first-line. This regimen should be considered when other options have failed. The guideline also mentions vemurafenib/cobimetinib + atezolizumab as an alternative triplet.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Vemurafenib/Cobimetinib + Atezolizumab (second-line, useful in certain circumstances, BRAF V600+)

trial_data.results: Improved PFS vs placebo + vem/cobi; OS benefit at second interim analysis

tier_1_regimens.notes: This triplet regimen is recommended for second-line therapy despite FDA approval in first-line. The NCCN panel considers the toxicity of triple combination to be excessive for first-line use. It may be considered for selected patients who have exhausted other options. The guideline states 'excessive toxicity with minimal additive benefit' as rationale.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Imatinib (second-line, useful in certain circumstances, KIT mutation positive)

trial_data.trial_name: Hodi et al. JCO 2013, Carvajal et al. JAMA 2011
trial_data.results: ORR 17-30% in KIT-mutated; better in exon 11/13 mutations

tier_1_regimens.notes: Same as entry above for imatinib. The guidelines also list nilotinib, dasatinib, and ripretinib as useful in certain circumstances for KIT-mutated melanoma. KIT amplifications have minimal sensitivity. Testing for KIT mutations should be considered in appropriate clinical settings (acral, mucosal, chronic sun-damaged skin).

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Binimetinib (second-line, useful in certain circumstances, NRAS mutation positive)

trial_data.results: ORR 15%; modest PFS improvement vs dacarbazine; no OS benefit

tier_1_regimens.notes: Binimetinib is a useful option for NRAS-mutated melanoma after progression on immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. It is category 2B. The NEMO trial showed a response rate of 15% and modest PFS improvement but no OS benefit compared to dacarbazine. This is not a preferred option due to limited efficacy.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Cytotoxic agents (second-line, useful in certain circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cytotoxic agents are reserved for patients who are not candidates for further standard immune-based, BRAF/MEK inhibitor, or clinical trial-directed therapy and who have symptomatic cancer. Carboplatin/paclitaxel and temozolomide are preferred among cytotoxic options. Other agents include dacarbazine, paclitaxel, albumin-bound paclitaxel, and CVD (category 2B). Multiagent chemotherapy shows marginal improvement in response rate over single-agent dacarbazine with no OS benefit. These are all category 2A (except CVD 2B) under 'Useful in Certain Circumstances'.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC) (intralesional, for unresectable CSIT, preferred)
tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line or subsequent for unresectable stage III CSIT

drugs.name: Talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC)
drugs.schedule: Multiple injections (up to 6 doses per course)

tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Preferred (for unresectable CSIT); Other Recommended (for limited resectable)

trial_data.results: DRR 16.3% vs 2.1% for GM-CSF; ORR 26.4% vs 5.7%; CR 11% vs <1%; OS benefit not significant overall; greatest benefit in stage IIIB/IIC and IV-M1a

tier_1_regimens.notes: T-VEC is category 1 for unresectable stage III clinical satellite/in-transit disease. It is also an option for limited resectable CSIT (as neoadjuvant therapy or alone). Response rate is highest in patients with less advanced disease (stage IIIB/C) and those who are treatment-naïve. T-VEC can also be used for accessible lesions in stage IV disease (M1a). Common toxicities include injection-site reactions and flu-like symptoms. T-VEC is also used as neoadjuvant therapy followed by surgery; the phase II trial showed improved RFS at 2 years (29.5% vs 16.5%) compared to surgery alone.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Interleukin-2 (intralesional, for unresectable CSIT, useful in certain circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for unresectable CSIT

trial_data.trial_name: Multiple phase II and retrospective studies
trial_data.results: Complete response in injected lesions 67-96%; partial responses also common; no response in uninjected lesions

tier_1_regimens.notes: Intralesional IL-2 is a category 2B option for unresectable CSIT disease when T-VEC is not available or appropriate. It has high complete response rates in injected lesions (especially dermal), but minimal bystander effect on uninjected lesions. Toxicity is local (injection site reaction, swelling) and systemic (flu-like symptoms) but generally grade 1-2. This should be used at centers with expertise.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Melphalan-based isolated limb infusion/perfusion (ILI/ILP) (regional therapy for limb-only CSIT)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for limb-only CSIT

drugs.name: Melphalan
drugs.schedule: Single procedure, sometimes with actinomycin D or TNF-alfa

trial_data.endpoints: ORR, complete response rate, limb salvage
trial_data.results: ILP ORR ~90% (median), CR ~58%; ILI ORR ~73%, CR ~33%; median CR rates vary by regimen

tier_1_regimens.notes: Isolated limb perfusion/infusion is a regional therapy option for patients with recurrent melanoma confined to a limb who have progressed on or have contraindications to standard therapies. This procedure should only be done at centers with experience. ILP is more invasive but may have higher response rates; ILI is simpler and can be repeated. Both use melphalan-based regimens (with or without actinomycin D or TNF-alfa). The guidelines note that with the advent of more effective systemic therapy, ILI/ILP is increasingly considered as first-line treatment for regionally recurrent melanoma limited to an extremity.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Intrathecal + intravenous Nivolumab (for leptomeningeal disease, category 2B)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for leptomeningeal involvement

trial_data.trial_name: Glitza Oliva et al. Nat Med 2023; Zhen et al. J Neurooncol 2024
trial_data.results: May be considered for patients on stable or decreasing doses of dexamethasone (≤4 mg)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Patients with leptomeningeal involvement may be candidates for concurrent intrathecal and intravenous nivolumab (category 2B). This should only be considered in patients on stable or decreasing doses of dexamethasone ≤4 mg. The evidence is from two referenced studies but the guideline does not provide further detail. This is a highly specialized treatment option.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Bevacizumab (for radiation necrosis, useful in certain circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.site: Cutaneous Melanoma (brain metastases with radiation necrosis)

drugs.name: Bevacizumab
drugs.schedule: Short course to reduce steroid dose and improve symptoms

trial_data.endpoints: Reduction of radiation necrosis, steroid taper
trial_data.results: Bevacizumab may allow improvement in quality of life by reducing steroid dose and improving functional status

tier_1_regimens.notes: Bevacizumab can be used for symptomatic radiation necrosis after SRS that is not responsive to corticosteroids. It may also be used to lower steroid dose in patients refractory to steroid withdrawal. Allow washout of at least 2 weeks before surgery. Risks of anti-VEGF therapy in the setting of melanoma metastases with hemorrhage should be weighed against benefit.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph (subcutaneous, alternative to IV pembrolizumab)

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph
drugs.schedule: Different dosing and administration compared to IV pembrolizumab

tier_1_regimens.notes: Subcutaneous pembrolizumab formulation may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab. It has different dosing and administration instructions. The guideline states that this is for nivolumab monotherapy substitution; for nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy, it is not approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab. Similarly, atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs may substitute for IV atezolizumab.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy (subcutaneous, alternative to IV nivolumab)

drugs.name: Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy
drugs.schedule: Different dosing and administration compared to IV nivolumab

tier_1_regimens.notes: Subcutaneous nivolumab may be substituted for IV nivolumab. Not approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab. Dosing and administration differ from IV formulation.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs (subcutaneous, alternative to IV atezolizumab)

drugs.name: Atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs
drugs.schedule: Different dosing and administration compared to IV infusion

tier_1_regimens.notes: Subcutaneous atezolizumab may be substituted for IV atezolizumab. This is applicable when atezolizumab is used (e.g., in triplet regimens for BRAF-mutant melanoma).

tier_2_handbook.definition: Cutaneous melanoma is a malignant neoplasm arising from melanocytes, pigment-producing cells of neural crest origin that normally reside in the basal layer of the epidermis and in hair follicles. It can occur on any skin surface, including sun-exposed and sun-protected sites. The AJCC 8th Edition staging system stratifies cutaneous melanoma by Breslow thickness, ulceration, and nodal involvement, with 5-year disease-specific survival (DSS) exceeding 96% for stage IA (T1a N0 M0) and ranging from 75% to 94% for localized melanomas >1.0 mm thick (stage IIC 5-year DSS ~75%) [28]. For patients with regional nodal metastases, 5-year DSS ranges from 24% to 93% depending on nodal burden, ulceration, and other factors [28]. Historically, 5-year survival for distant metastatic disease was <10%, but with modern immune checkpoint and targeted therapies, long-term remission is achievable in ~50% of patients [35]. Three clinical subtypes are recognized based on cumulative sun damage (CSD): low-CSD (superficial spreading and nodular), high-CSD (lentigo maligna type), and acral melanoma (palms, soles, subungual) [MS-2]. Mucosal, uveal, and leptomeningeal melanomas are distinct diseases with different genetic profiles and natural histories and are covered in separate NCCN guidelines [36,37]. The incidence of cutaneous melanoma has risen steadily, driven largely by diagnoses of in situ and thin (T1) lesions, raising concerns about histopathologic overinterpretation [2]. The thickest (T4) melanomas continue to increase, particularly among individuals with lower socioeconomic status and skin of color [2].

subtypes.name: Superficial spreading melanoma (SSM)
subtypes.description: Most common histologic subtype, typically arising in intermittently sun-exposed skin. Characterized by a radial growth phase before vertical invasion.
subtypes.key_associations: Intermittent sun exposure, younger age, BRAF V600E mutations (~80% of BRAF-mutant SSM), often associated with pre-existing nevus

subtypes.name: Nodular melanoma (NM)
subtypes.description: Aggressive subtype with rapid vertical growth; often diagnosed at thicker Breslow depth. Minimal or no radial growth phase.
subtypes.key_associations: Older age, male sex, trunk or head/neck location, BRAF mutations, higher mitotic rate, poorer prognosis per unit thickness

subtypes.name: Lentigo maligna melanoma (LMM)
subtypes.description: Arises in chronically sun-damaged skin, usually on the head and neck of older individuals. Begins as lentigo maligna (in situ) and may progress to invasive melanoma.
subtypes.key_associations: Chronic sun exposure, older age (>60 years), low BRAF mutation rate (<30%), frequent KIT mutations, high CSD phenotype (marked solar elastosis)

subtypes.name: Acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM)
subtypes.description: Occurs on non-hair-bearing skin of palms, soles, and nail beds. Not related to UV exposure. Often diagnosed at later stage.
subtypes.frequency: 2–8% in white populations, higher in individuals with darker skin (up to 50%)
subtypes.key_associations: No UV association, KIT mutations (10–15%), NRAS mutations (15–20%), BRAF mutations rare, often with copy number alterations, more common in ethnic minorities

subtypes.name: Desmoplastic melanoma (DM)
subtypes.description: Uncommon subtype characterized by spindle cells and dense stromal fibrosis. Pure DM (≥90% desmoplasia) has lower SLN positivity (<5%) compared to mixed DM.
subtypes.key_associations: Chronic sun-damaged skin (head/neck), older age, neurotropism/perineural invasion, high local recurrence risk, low SLN positivity rate in pure DM, often BRAF wild-type, may harbor NF1 mutations

subtypes.name: BRAF V600-mutant melanoma
subtypes.description: Melanomas harboring an activating mutation in BRAF codon V600, most commonly V600E (80%) or V600K (15%). Sensitive to BRAF and MEK inhibitors.
subtypes.key_associations: Younger age, trunk location, intermittent sun exposure, higher nevi count, brisk response to targeted therapy, may have slightly lower response to anti-PD-1 but still benefits

subtypes.name: NRAS-mutant melanoma
subtypes.description: Activating mutations in NRAS (most commonly Q61R/L) drive MAPK and PI3K signaling. Associated with poorer survival in localized and advanced stages.
subtypes.key_associations: Chronic or intermittent sun exposure, older age, thick primaries, no approved targeted therapy (MEK inhibitors produce modest responses), low probability of concurrent BRAF or KIT mutations

subtypes.name: KIT-mutant melanoma
subtypes.description: Mutations in KIT occur primarily in mucosal, acral, and chronically sun-damaged skin subtypes. Activating mutations in exons 11, 13, 17 confer sensitivity to KIT inhibitors.
subtypes.frequency: 10–15% of mucosal/acral melanomas; 2–3% of CSD melanomas; extremely rare in intermittent sun-exposed skin
subtypes.key_associations: Acral and mucosal sites, older age, UV-independent, response to imatinib/nilotinib varies by mutation type (exon 11/13 sensitive, exon 17 resistant)

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Cutaneous melanoma arises from the accumulation of genomic alterations that activate the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway and evade tumor suppression. The most frequent driver mutations occur in BRAF (45–50%), an intracellular serine-threonine kinase in the MAPK cascade. Approximately 80% are BRAF V600E, 15% V600K, and 5% V600R/M/D/G [44,146-148]. BRAF V600 mutations confer constitutive activation of MEK/ERK signaling, driving proliferation and survival. These mutations are sensitive to BRAF and MEK inhibitors, but combination therapy is superior to monotherapy [47,462-464]. NRAS mutations occur in 15–20% of melanomas, most commonly Q61R/L, and also activate MAPK signaling (via RAF-MEK-ERK) and PI3K-AKT pathways [59,60]. NRAS mutations are mutually exclusive with BRAF mutations and are associated with poor prognosis in localized and advanced disease [60]. KIT mutations are found in 10–15% of mucosal and acral melanomas and 2–3% of chronically sun-damaged skin melanomas; they involve receptor tyrosine kinase signaling and respond variably to imatinib and nilotinib [54-58]. Activating KIT mutations in exons 11 (e.g., L576P, W557R) and 13 (K642E) are sensitive, whereas exon 17 mutations (e.g., D816H) are resistant [55-58]. Melanomas also harbor TERT promoter mutations (in up to 70%), leading to telomerase reactivation, and CDKN2A deletions/losses (9p21) in ~50%, disrupting cell cycle control [171,172]. PTEN loss and PI3K/AKT pathway alterations are common in BRAF-mutant melanomas and contribute to resistance [171]. Other less frequent drivers include NF1 mutations (in desmoplastic and CSD melanomas), RAC1 P29S mutations, and gene fusions involving BRAF, NTRK1/2/3, ROS1, ALK (each <1%) [62,63,52,53]. The tumor mutational burden (TMB) is typically high in cutaneous melanoma due to ultraviolet (UV) signature mutations (C>T transitions), which correlate with response to immune checkpoint inhibitors [72-74]. PD-L1 expression is variable and not currently recommended for treatment selection [71]. Chromosomal aberrations such as gains in 7q (BRAF) and losses in 9p (CDKN2A) are common [105]. Somatic molecular testing for BRAF is recommended for patients with stage III or IV disease for targeted therapy; multigene panel testing should be considered if negative for actionable mutations [ME-C 2-3 of 8].

epidemiology.incidence: In 2026, an estimated 112,000 patients will be diagnosed with cutaneous melanoma in the United States [1]. Incidence has steadily increased over several decades, largely driven by diagnoses of melanoma in situ and thin (T1) tumors, raising concerns about histopathologic overinterpretation [2]. However, the thickest melanomas (T4) continue to rise, particularly among individuals with lower socioeconomic status and skin of color [2]. Cutaneous melanoma is the fifth most common cancer in men and women in the U.S.
epidemiology.mortality: Approximately 8,510 deaths are expected in 2026 [1]. Survival strongly correlates with stage at diagnosis: 5-year disease-specific survival (DSS) is >96% for stage IA, decreases to 75–94% for localized T2-T4 primaries, and ranges from 24–93% for stage III depending on nodal burden and ulceration [28]. Historical 5-year survival for stage IV disease was <10% with a median survival <1 year, but modern systemic therapies have improved outcomes, with long-term remission achievable in ~50% of patients [31-35].
epidemiology.trends: Incidence rates have increased, with the majority of new cases being early-stage disease. Mortality rates have declined in younger populations over the past decade due to advances in therapy, but disparities persist by race, ethnicity, and socioeconomic status. T4 melanomas continue to increase in incidence, particularly in individuals with lower socioeconomic status and/or skin of color [2].
epidemiology.demographics: Melanoma occurs most often in non-Hispanic white individuals with lighter skin tones, but can occur in people of any racial or ethnic group, including in areas without substantial sun exposure (palms, soles, nails). Male sex and age >50 years are independent risk factors [1]. The median age at diagnosis is 65 years. In the U.S., melanoma incidence is higher in whites compared to Black, Hispanic, and Asian populations, but mortality is disproportionately higher among Black men with acral melanoma [2,22]. The risk of second primary melanoma is increased in patients with a prior melanoma, particularly in males and older patients [62,63].

risk_factors.detail: Higher incidence and mortality compared to females; male sex is a risk factor for single and multiple primary melanomas [1].
Risk increases with age; most cases are diagnosed in individuals over 50 [1].
Clinically atypical nevi serve as risk markers for melanoma elsewhere on the body; higher overall nevus count and large nevi increase risk [2,3,6].
Light skin tone, tendency to sunburn, red hair, blue eyes, Fitzpatrick skin type I, pheomelanin-predominant phenotype [6,52,53].
Especially during childhood and adolescence; intermittent intense sun exposure is a key modifiable risk factor [3,4,7].
Indoor tanning (UVA/UVB devices) significantly increases melanoma risk, particularly with earlier age of first use and greater cumulative exposure [6,19,20].

risk_factors.factor: Residence in sunnier climate/latitude nearer equator
risk_factors.detail: Higher ambient UV radiation correlates with increased incidence, especially for high-CSD melanomas [20,21].
Prior non-melanoma skin cancer is associated with a 2- to 3-fold increased risk of subsequent melanoma due to shared UV etiology [55,56].
Solid organ transplantation, hematopoietic cell transplantation, HIV/AIDS, and chronic lymphocytic leukemia/small lymphocytic lymphoma confer increased risk [3,6,7,8,11,12,13].
risk_factors.strength: Moderate to strong for transplant recipients

risk_factors.detail: Defective DNA repair leads to extreme UV sensitivity; 5% of patients develop melanoma, often at young age [14].

risk_factors.factor: Germline pathogenic variants (CDKN2A, CDK4, MC1R, BAP1, TERT, POT1, MITF)
risk_factors.detail: High-penetrance variants associated with familial melanoma; CDKN2A accounts for 20–40% of familial cases. Other cancer predisposition genes (TP53, PTEN, CHEK2, BRCA1/2, BLM, ATM) increase risk [3,15-17,66].
risk_factors.strength: Strong for CDKN2A/CDK4/BAP1; moderate for others

risk_factors.detail: Two or more invasive melanomas in first-degree relatives significantly increase risk; risk of second primary melanoma is higher in familial cases [62,63,65].
Linked to truncal and extremity melanomas (low-CSD); meta-analysis shows RR = 1.6 for intermittent vs. chronic exposure [6,22,24].
Associated with head/neck melanomas (lentigo maligna type, high-CSD) [3,24].
Survivors of childhood cancer have 2.5-fold increased risk of subsequent melanoma [5,10].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Cutaneous melanoma typically presents as a new or changing pigmented skin lesion, often detected by the patient or during a routine skin examination. The classic presentation follows the ABCDE (asymmetry, border irregularity, color variegation, diameter >6 mm, evolution) criteria. Most melanomas arise de novo, though up to 20% may be associated with a preexisting nevus [ME-A 1 of 2]. Lesions are most common on the trunk in males and on the lower extremities in females, but can occur anywhere, including sun-protected sites (palms, soles, subungual) [MS-2]. The clinical subtype varies: low cumulative sun damage (CSD) melanomas (superficial spreading, nodular) present on intermittently sun-exposed skin; high CSD melanomas (lentigo maligna) occur on chronically sun-damaged skin, typically on the head and neck in older individuals; acral melanomas arise on non-hair-bearing surfaces (palms, soles, nail beds) and are unrelated to sun exposure [MS-3, ME-B 2 of 3]. Patients may also present with regional lymphadenopathy or in-transit metastases (cutaneous or subcutaneous nodules between the primary site and regional nodal basin) as the first manifestation. A small proportion present with distant metastatic disease, often with symptoms referable to the involved organ (e.g., brain, lung, liver) [ME-9]. Melanoma in situ (MIS) appears as a slowly enlarging, irregularly pigmented macule, often on sun-damaged skin; the lentigo maligna subtype can reach several centimeters in diameter [ME-E 1 of 3].

symptoms.name: Change in a preexisting mole
symptoms.detail: Evolution in size, shape, color, or elevation over weeks to months
symptoms.alarm: Yes – any change in a mole warrants evaluation

symptoms.name: New pigmented lesion
symptoms.detail: Appearance of a new spot, especially in adults
symptoms.alarm: Yes – new lesion after age 30 is suspicious

symptoms.name: Itching or tenderness
symptoms.detail: Localized pruritus or pain at the lesion site
symptoms.alarm: Moderate – can be associated with inflammation or ulceration

symptoms.name: Bleeding or ulceration
symptoms.detail: Spontaneous bleeding, crusting, or non-healing sore
symptoms.frequency: Less common, more frequent in advanced lesions

symptoms.name: Lymph node swelling
symptoms.detail: Palpable regional lymph nodes, often firm or matted
symptoms.frequency: Variable – present in stage III disease

symptoms.name: Neurological symptoms
symptoms.detail: Headache, seizure, focal deficits from brain metastases
symptoms.frequency: Occurs in stage IV with CNS involvement

signs.name: Asymmetry, border irregularity, color variegation, diameter >6 mm, evolution
signs.detail: ABCDE criteria – highly suspicious for melanoma

signs.name: Ulceration
signs.detail: Loss of epidermal integrity over the lesion, indicating deep invasion
signs.frequency: Associated with thicker tumors (T2b and above)

signs.name: Elevation or nodularity
signs.detail: Palpable nodule within or under the lesion

signs.name: Satellite or in-transit metastases
signs.detail: Cutaneous or subcutaneous nodules within 2 cm (satellite) or between primary and nodal basin (in-transit)

signs.name: Regional lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Palpable, enlarged, firm lymph nodes in the draining basin

signs.name: Dermatoscopic features
signs.detail: Pigment network, atypical dots/globules, regression structures, blue-white veil

atypical_or_missed: Amelanotic melanoma – presents as a pink, red, or flesh-colored nodule, often mistaken for basal cell carcinoma or benign lesion
Desmoplastic melanoma – often presents as a firm, scar-like plaque, frequently on sun-damaged skin of the head and neck, and may be misdiagnosed as scar or dermatofibroma [ME-1A]
Acral lentiginous melanoma – occurs on palms, soles, or subungual sites, often presenting as a dark streak or patch, can be mistaken for fungal infection or hematoma [ME-E 1 of 3]
Subungual melanoma (Hutchinson's sign) – pigmentation of the nail fold, may be misdiagnosed as trauma or onychomycosis
Mucosal melanoma – occurs on mucosal membranes (oral, anorectal, vulvovaginal), often diagnosed late due to hidden location [MS-3]
Melanoma in situ (lentigo maligna) – slow-growing, large, irregular patch on sun-damaged skin; may be clinically subtle and misdiagnosed as solar lentigo or pigmented actinic keratosis
Regression – partial or complete loss of pigmentation in a melanoma, may lead to underestimation of Breslow thickness
Primary melanoma of unknown primary – presents as nodal or distant metastasis without an identifiable primary lesion; requires thorough dermatologic exam [ME-6A]

red_flags: Rapid growth or change in a preexisting nevus within weeks to months [ME-1]
Ulceration or bleeding from a pigmented lesion [ME-1A]
Asymmetric lesion with irregular borders and multiple colors [ME-1]
Diameter >6 mm (though smaller melanomas can occur) [ME-1]
New pigmented lesion in an adult, especially after age 30 [ME-1]
Elevated or nodular component, especially with ulceration [ME-1A]
Itching, pain, or tenderness at a mole site [ME-1]
Palpable regional lymphadenopathy, particularly if firm, matted, or enlarging [ME-1]
Cutaneous or subcutaneous nodules adjacent to a melanoma scar or along lymphatic drainage (satellite/in-transit metastases) [ME-7]
Neurologic symptoms (headache, seizure, weakness) in a patient with melanoma – suspicious for brain metastases [ME-9, ME-J 1 of 5]
Unexplained weight loss, fatigue, or systemic symptoms in a melanoma patient – consider visceral metastases [ME-9]
Elevated serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) in the setting of suspected or confirmed metastatic disease – associated with worse prognosis [ME-9, ST-3]
Breslow thickness >1 mm, especially with ulceration or high mitotic rate (≥2/mm²) – higher risk of nodal and distant spread [ME-1A, ME-2]
Lymphovascular invasion or microsatellitosis on pathology – indicates aggressive biology [ME-1A, ME-4A]
Age ≤42 years, head/neck location, lymphovascular invasion, and/or mitotic index ≥2/mm² in thin melanomas (T1a with Breslow >0.5 mm) – additive risk for SLN positivity [ME-2]
Positive sentinel lymph node biopsy – upstages to stage III and portends decreased melanoma-specific survival [ME-2A, ME-5A]

History of multiple primary melanomas or strong family history of melanoma (especially CDKN2A, BAP1, CDK4 germline variants) – warrants genetic counseling and intensified surveillance [ME-12, ME-A 1 of 2]

diagnostic.test: Full-thickness skin biopsy (excisional, punch, saucerization, or incisional)
diagnostic.rationale: Gold standard to confirm diagnosis and microstage primary melanoma
diagnostic.key_findings: Provides Breslow thickness (mm), ulceration status, dermal mitotic rate (#/mm²), microsatellitosis, lymphovascular invasion, neurotropism, histologic subtype (desmoplastic, etc.), margin status. Excisional biopsy with 1-3 mm margins is preferred; shave biopsy may compromise microstaging but is acceptable for low suspicion [ME-B 1 of 3].

diagnostic.rationale: Aid in histopathologic diagnosis of equivocal lesions and for sentinel node evaluation
diagnostic.key_findings: Stains: S100, HMB45, Melan-A, SOX-10 for melanoma detection. IHC for PRAME can differentiate nodal nevi from metastases. D2-40 (lymphatic marker) helps distinguish lymphovascular invasion from microsatellites [ME-B 2 of 3, ME-F 4 of 5].

diagnostic.test: Molecular testing (CGH, FISH, GEP) for diagnostically challenging lesions
diagnostic.rationale: Differentiate benign from malignant melanocytic neoplasms when histopathology is equivocal
diagnostic.key_findings: Comparative genomic hybridization (CGH) or fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) can detect copy number changes typical of melanoma. Gene expression profiling (GEP) may be used but is not recommended for routine prognostication [ME-C 1 of 8].

diagnostic.test: BRAF V600 mutation testing (IHC or molecular)
diagnostic.rationale: Required to guide targeted therapy with BRAF/MEK inhibitors in stage III (adjuvant) or stage IV (unresectable/metastatic) disease
diagnostic.key_findings: BRAF V600E (80%), V600K (15%), others (5%). IHC using VE1 antibody is a rapid screen but requires confirmatory molecular testing (PCR or NGS) for negative or equivocal results. Testing on metastatic tissue is preferred over primary lesion if adequate material is available [ME-C 2 of 8, ME-C 3 of 8].

diagnostic.rationale: Considered for patients with advanced melanoma of acral, mucosal, or chronically sun-damaged skin origin who are being considered for KIT inhibitor therapy
diagnostic.key_findings: KIT mutations found in 10-15% of mucosal/acral melanomas, 2-3% of chronic sun-damaged skin. Imatinib and other KIT inhibitors show activity, particularly for exon 11 and 13 mutations. Exon 17 mutations (e.g., D816H) are resistant [ME-C 2 of 8].

diagnostic.rationale: Identify targetable alterations beyond BRAF/KIT (e.g., NRAS, NTRK fusions, ALK, ROS1) for clinical trial eligibility or targeted therapy
diagnostic.key_findings: Recommended if single-gene testing is negative or if broader profiling may guide treatment. Can detect BRAF non-V600 mutations and gene fusions [ME-C 3 of 8, ME-C 5 of 8].

diagnostic.rationale: Emerging tool for detecting minimal residual disease, monitoring response, and assessing prognosis in advanced and adjuvant settings
diagnostic.key_findings: Detectable ctDNA post-resection of stage III/IV correlates with worse prognosis. Serial assessments may be used for response monitoring in metastatic/neoadjuvant settings. Negative ctDNA does not rule out disease; tissue confirmation needed. Role in surveillance still being defined [ME-10, ME-C 3 of 8].

diagnostic.rationale: Prognostic marker in stage IV disease; part of AJCC staging for M category
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated LDH (M1c(1)) indicates worse prognosis regardless of metastatic site. Use at initial diagnosis of stage IV and for monitoring [ME-9, ST-3].

staging.test: Complete history and physical examination (H&P) with emphasis on locoregional area and draining lymph nodes
staging.rationale: Essential for clinical staging and detection of nodal or in-transit disease
Detect synchronous primary melanomas or suspicious nevi

staging.test: Cross-sectional imaging: chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with IV contrast (and neck CT or MRI if clinically indicated) and/or whole-body FDG-PET/CT
staging.rationale: Baseline staging to detect occult distant metastases. Also used for surveillance in high-risk patients
staging.indication: Stage IIIA (consider): to evaluate specific signs/symptoms or prior to adjuvant therapy. Stage IIIB/C/D and stage IV: recommended for baseline staging. Stage I-II: not routinely recommended unless needed for surgical planning or prior to adjuvant therapy for stage IIB/IIC. For surveillance in stage IIB-IV NED: consider every 3-12 mo for 2 yrs, then 6-12 mo for 3 yrs (category 2B) [ME-D 2 of 5, ME-D 4 of 5].

staging.test: Brain MRI with and without IV contrast (preferred) or CT if MRI contraindicated
staging.rationale: Detection of CNS metastases. Baseline for stage IIIB/C/D and stage IV; for surveillance in high-risk patients (e.g., prior brain metastases, stage IIIB without CNS metastases up to 3 years)
staging.indication: Stage IV: baseline. Stage IIIB/C/D: consider baseline (especially with high risk). Stage I-II: only to evaluate specific signs/symptoms [ME-D 2 of 5, ME-D 4 of 5].

staging.rationale: Assess regional lymph nodes for metastatic involvement; used for equivocal physical exam, SLNB alternative if not performed, or surveillance for positive SLNB without CLND
staging.indication: Consider prior to SLNB if equivocal exam; preferred for surveillance of positive SLNB if CLND not performed. Not a substitute for SLNB [ME-D 1 of 5, ME-D 2 of 5].

staging.rationale: Pathologic staging of regional nodal basin in clinically node-negative patients with appropriate risk factors
staging.indication: Discuss/offer for stage IB (T1b with ulceration or 0.8-1.0 mm) and stage II (T2a or higher). For T1a with adverse features (age ≤42, head/neck, LVI, mitotic rate ≥2/mm², Breslow >0.5 mm). Not routinely for T1a without adverse features. Discuss MRI-based risk if <5% probability [ME-2, ME-F 2 of 5].

staging.test: Lymph node biopsy (FNA or core biopsy) of palpable nodes
staging.indication: Clinically positive node(s). Core biopsy preferred to preserve tissue for molecular testing [ME-6].

staging.test: Biopsy of suspicious metastases (core, FNA, incisional, or excisional)
staging.rationale: Confirm stage IV disease and obtain tissue for molecular testing (BRAF, KIT, MGPT)
staging.indication: Initial presentation with stage IV disease or clinical recurrence [ME-9, ME-17A].

staging.rationale: Prognostic stratification in stage IV (M1a(0/1), M1b(0/1), M1c(0/1), M1d(0/1))
staging.indication: At diagnosis of stage IV and for disease monitoring [ME-9, ST-3].

biomarkers.rationale: Essential for selection of BRAF/MEK targeted therapy in stage III (adjuvant) and stage IV (unresectable/metastatic) disease
biomarkers.when: Recommended for stage III melanoma (high risk) and stage IV melanoma (or recurrence) when considering targeted therapy. Not recommended for resected stage I-II unless for clinical trial [ME-C 1 of 8].

biomarkers.rationale: Identify patients with advanced melanoma who may benefit from KIT inhibitors (imatinib, nilotinib). Most relevant for acral, mucosal, or chronic sun-damaged subtypes
biomarkers.when: Consider in appropriate clinical setting (e.g., mucosal, acral, or chronic sun-damaged primary) for advanced disease when considering targeted therapy [ME-C 2 of 8].

biomarkers.rationale: Detect uncommon targetable alterations (e.g., NTRK fusions, ROS1, ALK, BRAF non-V600) for clinical trial eligibility or off-label therapy
biomarkers.when: Recommended if BRAF single-gene test is negative and test results might guide future treatment decisions or clinical trial eligibility. Also for patients with advanced disease and no actionable mutation on standard testing [ME-C 3 of 8].

biomarkers.rationale: May provide prognostic information but should not guide clinical decision-making for immunotherapy selection
biomarkers.when: Not recommended for routine use. Utility requires further investigation [ME-C 4 of 8].

biomarkers.rationale: May correlate with response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, but use to guide treatment decisions remains investigational
biomarkers.when: Not recommended for routine use. Use only in clinical trials [ME-C 5 of 8].

biomarkers.rationale: Rapid screening test for BRAF V600E mutation; can be used for prompt initiation of targeted therapy in symptomatic/rapidly progressing patients
biomarkers.when: Acceptable as initial screen, but confirmatory molecular testing (PCR/NGS) is encouraged, especially if IHC negative. Sensitivity 89.2%, specificity 96.2% [ME-C 3 of 8].

biomarkers.rationale: Potential for minimal residual disease detection, response monitoring, and prognosis assessment
biomarkers.when: Role still being defined. Not recommended for routine clinical decision-making outside of clinical trials. Could be integrated into serial response assessment during systemic therapy [ME-10, ME-C 3 of 8].

biomarkers.test: Gene expression profiling (GEP) for SLNB risk prediction
biomarkers.rationale: May support shared decision-making for SLNB in select patients with T1b ± T2a melanoma who would decline SLNB if risk <10%. A high-risk result portends 3x higher risk of SLN involvement. Not recommended for other GEP tests [ME-C 1 of 8, ME-F 2 of 5].
biomarkers.when: Consider in select patients with T1b ± T2a when true risk <10% and patient/provider would decide against SLNB. Routine use in other settings not recommended [ME-2A, ME-2B].

biomarkers.test: Prognostic GEP tests for risk stratification
biomarkers.rationale: Not recommended. Current data do not support clinical utility beyond standard clinicopathologic factors
biomarkers.when: Do not incorporate into routine melanoma care. Use only in clinical trials [ME-2A, ME-C 1 of 8].

do_not_miss: SLNB in eligible patients with stage IB or II melanoma (T1b and higher) to provide accurate pathologic staging and guide adjuvant therapy decisions [ME-2, ME-F 2 of 5]
BRAF mutation testing for stage III and IV disease to identify candidates for targeted therapy [ME-4, ME-5, ME-6, ME-7, ME-9]
Serum LDH at diagnosis of stage IV disease for prognostic stratification [ME-9, ST-3]
Cross-sectional imaging (CT or PET/CT) for baseline staging in stage III and IV disease [ME-D 2 of 5]
Brain MRI for stage IV disease and for stage IIIB/C/D with high risk of CNS recurrence [ME-D 2 of 5]
avoid: Routine imaging (CT, PET/CT, brain MRI) for asymptomatic patients with stage 0, IA, IIA disease as yield is very low and false positives can cause harm [ME-D 2 of 5, ME-D 4 of 5]
Routine blood tests (e.g., LDH, complete blood count, liver function tests) for surveillance of stage I-II melanoma; only use if clinically indicated [ME-10, ME-11]
BRAF testing or MGPT for primary cutaneous melanoma in stage I-II unless required to guide adjuvant therapy or clinical trial enrollment [ME-1A, ME-C 5 of 8]
Prognostic GEP testing (e.g., 31-gene profile) for routine risk stratification; not yet validated for clinical utility [ME-2A, ME-C 1 of 8]
PD-L1 testing to guide immunotherapy selection; not recommended [ME-C 4 of 8]
Routine use of chest x-ray for surveillance of high-risk stage II melanoma (CT preferred) [ME-D 4 of 5]
Nodal basin ultrasound as substitute for SLNB; it is an adjunct only [ME-D 2 of 5]
KIT IHC testing as substitute for molecular testing (PCR/NGS) for KIT mutations [ME-C 3 of 8]

t_stages.description: Primary tumor thickness cannot be assessed (e.g., diagnosis by curettage) [ST-1]
No evidence of primary tumor (e.g., unknown primary or completely regressed melanoma) [ST-1]
Tumor thickness ≤1.0 mm; ulceration status unknown or unspecified [ST-1]
Breslow thickness <0.8 mm with ulceration; OR thickness 0.8-1.0 mm with or without ulceration [ST-1]
Tumor thickness >1.0-2.0 mm; ulceration status unknown or unspecified [ST-1]
Tumor thickness >2.0-4.0 mm; ulceration status unknown or unspecified [ST-1]
Tumor thickness >4.0 mm; ulceration status unknown or unspecified [ST-1]

n_stages.description: Regional nodes not assessed (e.g., SLNB not performed, regional nodes previously removed for another reason). Exception: For pT1 cM0 melanoma with no clinically detected regional metastases, assign cN0 instead of pNX [ST-2]
One tumor-involved node OR in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases with no tumor-involved nodes [ST-2]
One clinically occult (detected by SLN biopsy) [ST-2]
No regional lymph node disease; but presence of in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases [ST-2]
Two or three tumor-involved nodes OR in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases with one tumor-involved node [ST-2]
Two or three clinically occult (detected by SLN biopsy) [ST-2]
Two or three, at least one of which was clinically detected [ST-2]
One clinically occult or clinically detected node plus in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases [ST-2]

Four or more tumor-involved nodes OR in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases with two or more tumor-involved nodes OR any number of matted nodes with or without in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases [ST-2]
Four or more clinically occult (detected by SLN biopsy) [ST-2]
Four or more, at least one of which was clinically detected, OR presence of any number of matted nodes [ST-2]

Two or more clinically occult or clinically detected nodes AND presence of in-transit, satellite, and/or microsatellite metastases, OR any number of matted nodes with in-transit/satellite/microsatellite metastases [ST-2]

m_stages.description: Evidence of distant metastasis; LDH level must be specified as recorded (no suffix), not elevated (0), or elevated (1) [ST-3]
Distant metastasis to skin, soft tissue (including muscle), and/or nonregional lymph node [ST-3]
Distant metastasis to lung with or without M1a sites of disease [ST-3]
Distant metastasis to non-CNS visceral sites with or without M1a or M1b sites of disease [ST-3]
Distant metastasis to CNS with or without M1a, M1b, or M1c sites of disease [ST-3]

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Melanoma in situ; no invasive component; no evidence of regional or distant metastasis. Prognosis is excellent with complete excision. Five- and 10-year disease-specific survival (DSS) >99% [MS-2].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: >99% (approximate based on MS-2 statement for in situ)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (wide excision with 0.5-1 cm margins; alternatives include topical imiquimod or radiotherapy for lentigo maligna subtype in select patients) [ME-2, ME-E 1 of 3]

stage_groups.criteria: Clinical and pathological: T1a N0 M0 [ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Thin melanoma (<0.8 mm, nonulcerated). Very low risk of nodal (<5%) and distant metastasis. Five- and 10-year DSS >96% for patients with tumors ≤1.0 mm [MS-2].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: >96% (for T1 including both T1a and T1b)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (wide excision with 1 cm margins, category 1). SLNB generally not recommended due to <5% risk unless adverse features present [ME-2, ME-2A].

stage_groups.criteria: Clinical and pathological: T1b N0 M0 or T2a N0 M0 [ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Thin melanoma with ulceration or 0.8-1.0 mm thickness (T1b) or intermediate thickness 1.01-2.0 mm without ulceration (T2a). Risk of nodal positivity 5-10% for T1b. Five- and 10-year DSS for melanomas >1.0 mm: 75-94% depending on thickness, ulceration, and mitotic rate [MS-2].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (wide excision 1 cm for ≤1.0 mm, 1-2 cm for 1.01-2.0 mm). Discuss and consider SLNB for T1b (5-10% risk). Offer SLNB for T2a [ME-2, ME-2A, ME-E 1 of 3].

stage_groups.criteria: Clinical and pathological: T2b N0 M0 or T3a N0 M0 [ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Intermediate thickness 1.01-2.0 mm with ulceration (T2b) or thick 2.01-4.0 mm without ulceration (T3a). Risk of nodal positivity >10%. Survival decreases with increasing thickness and ulceration [MS-2].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated; localized melanomas >1.0 mm range 75-94%
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative. Wide excision (1-2 cm for 1.01-2.0 mm, 2 cm for >2.0 mm). SLNB offered (likelihood >10%) [ME-3, ME-3A].

stage_groups.criteria: Clinical and pathological: T3b N0 M0 or T4a N0 M0 [ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Thick melanoma 2.01-4.0 mm with ulceration (T3b) or very thick >4.0 mm without ulceration (T4a). High risk of locoregional and distant recurrence. Five-year DSS approximately 67-75% [MS-2].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Approximately 67-75% (extrapolated from MS-2: for localized melanomas >4.0 mm, 5-year DSS ~70-75%)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (wide excision 2 cm). SLNB offered. Adjuvant systemic therapy (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) may be considered after staging (category 1). Consider primary tumor site RT for high-risk local recurrence (category 2B) [ME-3, ME-3B].

stage_groups.criteria: Clinical and pathological: T4b N0 M0 [ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Very thick melanoma >4.0 mm with ulceration. Highest risk among stage II. Five-year DSS approximately 53-60% [MS-2].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Approximately 53-60% (extrapolated from MS-2 discussion for T4b)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (wide excision 2 cm). SLNB offered. Adjuvant systemic therapy (category 1) strongly considered after pathological staging. Consider primary tumor site RT (category 2B) [ME-3, ME-3B].

stage_groups.criteria: Clinical staging: Any T (including Tis) with N ≥1 and M0. Only one stage group for clinical Stage III melanoma [ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Regional lymph node metastasis (clinically detected) or in-transit/satellite/microsatellite metastases. Prognosis varies widely by nodal burden. Five- and 10-year DSS range from 24% to 93% depending on extent [MS-2].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative. Workup includes imaging to rule out distant disease. Treatment involves wide excision of primary (if present) and lymphadenectomy; may consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy for clinically node-positive disease [ME-6, ME-6A].

stage_groups.criteria: T1a/b, T2a; N1a or N2a; M0 [ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: One to three clinically occult nodes (detected by SLNB) in patients with thin to intermediate (nonulcerated) primary. Melanoma-specific survival 93% at 5 years (AJCC 8th Edition). Lower risk of recurrence; SLN tumor burden <0.3 mm may have survival similar to stage IB [ME-5A].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative. Wide excision + SLNB. Nodal basin surveillance (preferred over CLND). Consider adjuvant systemic therapy if SLN tumor burden ≥0.3 mm, especially if T1b-T2a/N1a or N2a; observation often appropriate for very low tumor burden [ME-5, ME-5A].

stage_groups.criteria: T0 (unknown primary) with N1b or N1c; T1a/b, T2a with N1b/c or N2b; T2b, T3a with N1a/b/c or N2a/b; all M0 [ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Includes varying combinations of primary tumor thickness and nodal/direct lymphatic metastasis. Patients with microsatellites alone (SLN-negative) have more favorable survival than SLN-positive [ME-4A]. Five-year melanoma-specific survival around 83% (AJCC 8th Edition) [MS-2].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Approximately 83% (extrapolated from MS-2 for stage IIIB)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative. Wide excision + CLND (or nodal surveillance if microsatellites only). Adjuvant systemic therapy (category 1 for node-positive, category 2A for microsatellites alone). Consider neoadjuvant therapy for clinically positive nodes [ME-4, ME-4A, ME-5, ME-6].

stage_groups.criteria: T0 with N2b/c or N3b/c; T1a/b, T2a/b, T3a with N2c or N3a/b/c; T3b, T4a with any N ≥ N1; T4b with N1a/b/c or N2a/b/c; all M0 [ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Bulkier nodal disease (≥2 clinically detected, or matted nodes, or any nodal with microsatellites/in-transit). High risk of recurrence and death. Five-year melanoma-specific survival approximately 69% (AJCC 8th Edition) [MS-2].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative but high-risk. Wide excision + CLND (if not done). Neoadjuvant systemic therapy preferred for clinically positive/resectable nodes. Adjuvant systemic therapy recommended (category 1). Consider nodal basin RT if high risk features (category 2B) [ME-5, ME-6, ADJSYS 1 of 2].

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Very thick, ulcerated primary with four or more involved nodes, matted nodes, and/or lymph node and in-transit/metastatic disease. Highest risk in stage III. Five-year melanoma-specific survival approximately 32% (AJCC 8th Edition) [MS-2].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative but extremely high risk. Multi-modality treatment: neoadjuvant systemic therapy (preferred), wide excision, CLND, adjuvant systemic therapy (category 1). Consider adjuvant RT to nodal basin (category 2B) [ME-6, ADJSYS 1 of 2].

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, any N, M1a(0) or M1a(1) [ST-3, ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Distant metastases to skin, soft tissue, and/or nonregional lymph nodes. Median OS historically <1 year but improved with modern therapies. Five-year survival approximately 10-15% before targeted/immune therapy; now long-term survival in ~50% of appropriately treated patients [MS-2].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Historically <10% at 5 years; current long-term survival ~50% (from MS-2 discussion for stage IV with effective therapy)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative but potentially prolonged remission. Systemic therapy is standard (immune checkpoint inhibitors, BRAF/MEK inhibitors for BRAF-mutant). Consider local therapy for oligometastasis (resection, SBRT, T-VEC). Best supportive care [ME-17, MELSYS 1 of 10].

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, any N, M1b(0) or M1b(1) [ST-3, ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Distant metastases to lung, with or without M1a sites. Five-year survival similar to M1a but with modern therapy comparable outcomes [MS-2].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative with potential for durable remission. Systemic therapy (immunotherapy or targeted therapy). Local treatments for symptomatic lung metastases [ME-17].

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, any N, M1c(0) or M1c(1) [ST-3, ST-4]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Non-CNS visceral metastases (liver, bone, pancreas, etc.) with or without M1a/b sites. Worse prognosis if LDH elevated. Five-year survival lower than M1a/b [MS-2].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Lower than M1a/b (exact numbers not provided)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative. Systemic therapy. Consider clinical trials. Palliative RT for symptomatic lesions [ME-17].

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, any N, M1d(0) or M1d(1) [ST-3, ST-4]
