stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Distant metastases to CNS (brain, leptomeninges) with or without other sites. Historically worst prognosis, but intracranial responses to immunotherapy and targeted therapy are improving outcomes. Leptomeningeal disease carries grave prognosis [ME-J 1 of 5].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Poor historically; improving with modern systemic and local therapies
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative with multidisciplinary approach. Brain-directed therapy (SRS, surgery, WBRT) and systemic therapy (nivolumab/ipilimumab preferred, BRAF/MEK for BRAF-mutant). Consider clinical trials [ME-17, ME-J 3 of 5].

staging_pearls: Clinical staging includes microstaging of the primary melanoma plus clinical/radiological/biopsy evaluation for metastases; pathological staging adds information from wide excision and regional lymph node evaluation (SLNB or CLND) [ST-4].
Mitotic rate is no longer part of T1 staging in AJCC 8th Edition, but remains an important prognostic factor and should still be reported in pathology [ME-1A].

Microsatellites, clinical satellites, and in-transit metastases are classified together as N1c/N2c/N3c based on number of tumor-involved regional lymph nodes (0, 1, ≥2 respectively). They upstage to at least stage IIIB [ST-2, ME-1A].
LDH status is incorporated into M1 subcategories: (0) for not elevated, (1) for elevated. Elevated LDH denotes worse prognosis and places patient in higher risk group [ST-3].

For pathological stage 0 and T1 without clinically detected regional or distant metastases (pTis/pT1 cN0 cM0), pathological evaluation of lymph nodes is not required; use cN0 to assign pathological stage [ST-4].

Patients with very low tumor volume stage IIIA (T1a/b-T2a/N1a or N2a) with SLN tumor deposits <0.3 mm have 5-year MSS similar to stage IB (T2aN0) and may be candidates for less intensive surveillance and observation rather than adjuvant therapy [ME-5A].
Five- and 10-year DSS for localized disease (stage I-II) is >96% for tumors ≤1.0 mm and 75-94% for melanomas >1.0 mm thickness depending on ulceration and mitotic rate [MS-2].
For stage III, clinically occult nodal disease (SLN-detected) has better prognosis than clinically evident nodal disease. Five-year DSS ranges from 93% (stage IIIA) to 32% (stage IIID) [MS-2, ST-4].

Stage IV survival has dramatically improved with modern targeted and immune therapies; long-term (5-year) survival now approximately 50% for patients treated with effective systemic therapy, compared to historical <10% [MS-2].

management_principles.overview: Cutaneous melanoma management requires a multidisciplinary, stage-specific approach integrating surgery, systemic therapy, and radiation therapy. In 2026, an estimated 112,000 new cases and 8,510 deaths are projected in the US [1]. Treatment philosophy emphasizes personalized shared decision-making, with consideration of tumor thickness, ulceration, mitotic rate, nodal status, BRAF mutation status, and patient factors. For early-stage disease (Stage 0–II), surgical excision with appropriate margins is curative for most, but adjuvant systemic therapy is increasingly considered for high-risk Stage IIB–III. For regionally advanced or metastatic disease, neoadjuvant and adjuvant immunotherapy or BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy have improved recurrence-free survival, though impact on overall survival remains under investigation [444–448]. Multimodal care integrates surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, dermatology, pathology, and nursing. Clinical trial participation is encouraged where available.

by_intent.approach: Wide excision with or without sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) for primary tumors ≥1 mm thickness or with adverse features. Complete lymph node dissection (CLND) is no longer routine for sentinel node-positive patients; nodal ultrasound surveillance is preferred [336,452]. Adjuvant systemic therapy (anti-PD-1 or BRAF/MEK inhibitors) is recommended for high-risk Stage III and may be considered for Stage IIB/IIC [445–448].

by_intent.patient_group: Stage III (clinically positive nodes) and select Stage IV oligometastatic
by_intent.approach: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is preferred for resectable stage III with clinical nodes, based on trials showing improved event-free survival [NEOSYS 1]. Options include ipilimumab + nivolumab (category 1 for initial presentation) or pembrolizumab [3,1]. Major pathologic response correlates with excellent outcomes; adjuvant therapy may be guided by response.

by_intent.patient_group: Unresectable Stage III or Stage IV
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy with immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1 ± ipilimumab) or BRAF/MEK inhibitors (if BRAF V600 mutant) is first-line [MELSYS 1]. Local therapies (radiation, intralesional T-VEC, isolated limb infusion) are options for symptom control. Best supportive care is reserved for poor performance status.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary tumor board review is strongly recommended for patients with extensive regional adenopathy, borderline resectable disease, brain metastases, or complex clinical scenarios. The team should include surgical oncologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, dermatopathologists, radiologists, and palliative care specialists [ME-J 1].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status is not explicitly graded in the guidelines, but ECOG 0–1 is required for most adjuvant and neoadjuvant trials. For patients with poor functional status or limited life expectancy, observation or less intensive regimens may be appropriate, especially for melanoma in situ or low-risk Stage IIIA [ME-2B, ADJSYS 2].

options.label: Wide excision with 0.5–1 cm margins (preferred)
options.recommendation: Wide excision is standard. For LM subtype, surgical excision is preferred but nonsurgical options may be considered.

options.label: Topical imiquimod 5% cream (for LM when surgery not possible or positive margins)
options.recommendation: Off-label use; phase 3 RADICAL trial showed comparable 95% response at 6 months vs radiotherapy [Hong AM, J Am Acad Dermatol 2025;93:1251-1260].

options.label: Radiation therapy (for LM when surgery not feasible)
options.recommendation: Definitive RT can be considered for medically inoperable patients [ME-H 1].

options.label: Active surveillance (for poor functional status/limited life expectancy)
options.recommendation: Shared decision-making; observation may be appropriate [ME-2B].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy needed after complete excision.

nodes.criteria: Stage IA: T1a (<0.8 mm, no ulceration) with risk of SLN positivity <5%

options.label: Wide excision with 1 cm margin (category 1)
options.recommendation: SLNB not recommended unless uncertainty about microstaging [ME-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation only.

nodes.criteria: Stage IB: T1b (<0.8 mm with ulceration or 0.8–1.0 mm ± ulceration) or T1a with adverse features (age ≤42, head/neck, LVI, mitotic rate ≥2/mm2). Risk of SLN positivity 5–10%.

options.label: Wide excision with 1 cm margin (category 1) and discuss/consider SLNB
options.recommendation: SLNB should be discussed and considered [ME-2]. Predictive GEP testing (e.g., Merlin Assay CP-GEP) may support shared decision-making if patient would decline SLNB for risk <10% [Hieken TJ, et al. JAMA Surg 2025;160:1358-1366].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No routine adjuvant therapy. If SLNB positive, proceed to Stage III pathway.

management_pathways.title: Stage IB (T2a) and Stage II (T2b or higher)

nodes.criteria: Clinical Stage IB (T2a) or Stage II (T2b–T4b), clinically node negative

options.label: Wide excision (category 1) with SLNB (discuss and offer)
options.recommendation: SLNB is recommended for T2b and higher; for T2a, discuss and offer. Pathologic staging strongly recommended before considering adjuvant therapy [ME-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: For pathologic Stage IIB/IIC after SLNB negative: observation (category 1) or adjuvant systemic therapy (nivolumab or pembrolizumab, category 1) and/or primary site RT (category 2B). Adjuvant PD-1 therapy is approved for Stage IIB/IIC. Shared decision-making essential [ADJSYS 1].

management_pathways.title: Stage III (Microscopic Satellites)
branching_basis: Presence of microsatellites in biopsy or wide excision

nodes.criteria: Microscopic satellites identified in initial biopsy or wide excision (defines at least Stage IIIB)

options.label: Wide excision (category 1) with SLNB (consider)
options.recommendation: SLNB should be considered if it will alter management [ME-4]. SLN positive upstages to at least IIIC.

options.label: Adjuvant systemic therapy (if SLN positive or high-risk)
options.recommendation: Observation is also an option. For microsatellite-positive, SLN-negative patients, adjuvant therapy trials may not be applicable [ME-4A].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Choice between observation and adjuvant systemic therapy based on overall risk (presence of SLN positivity, thickness, ulceration). If SLN negative and no other nodal disease, observation is reasonable given more favorable survival compared to SLN-positive [Bartlett EK, J Surg Oncol 2019;119:200-207].

nodes.criteria: Stage IIIA (T1a/b–T2a, N1a or N2a) with low tumor burden (<0.3 mm deposits)

options.label: Active nodal basin surveillance (preferred) ± imaging
options.recommendation: CLND not recommended; ultrasound surveillance is preferred. Adjuvant therapy may be considered if SLN deposits ≥0.3 mm [ME-5A]. Observation is acceptable for very low risk.

adjuvant_logic.summary: For Stage IIIA with <0.3 mm deposits, observation is often preferred due to favorable prognosis (5-year MSS similar to Stage IB). For ≥0.3 mm, consider adjuvant systemic therapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF+, all category 1) [ME-5, ADJSYS 1].

nodes.criteria: Stage IIIB/C/D (sentinel node positive) or Stage IIIA with higher risk features

options.label: Active nodal basin surveillance (preferred) vs CLND (select cases)
options.recommendation: Two phase III trials (MSLT-II, DeCOG) showed no MSS or OS benefit with CLND. CLND may be considered for high likelihood of additional positive nodes or inability to adhere to surveillance [ME-5A].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy is recommended (all category 1 for clinically positive nodes). Options: dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+, nivolumab, or pembrolizumab. For CSIT limited resectable, nivolumab is category 1 [ADJSYS 1].

options.label: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Preferred approach based on improved EFS. Regimens: ipilimumab + nivolumab (category 1 for initial presentation), pembrolizumab, or nivolumab [NEOSYS 1]. Followed by surgery ± adjuvant therapy guided by response.

options.label: Wide excision + therapeutic lymph node dissection (TLND)
options.recommendation: If neoadjuvant therapy not given or contraindicated. TLND should be anatomically complete [ME-G].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After TLND (if neoadjuvant not given): systemic therapy (preferred) and/or RT to nodal basin (category 2B) for high risk (extracapsular extension, non-response, number/size of nodes). After neoadjuvant + surgery: adjuvant therapy based on pathologic response (MPR may allow observation; non-MPR may continue systemic therapy) [NEOSYS 6].

options.label: Systemic therapy (as for Stage IV)
options.recommendation: Treat as Stage IV. If response, reassess resectability [ME-6A].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant; continue systemic therapy if NED after response.

options.label: Complete excision to clear margins
options.recommendation: Consider SLNB (category 2B) if will change treatment [ME-7].

options.label: Talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC)/intralesional therapy
options.recommendation: Neoadjuvant T-VEC improved RFS at 2 years (29.5% vs 16.5%) compared to surgery alone [Dummer R, et al. Nat Med 2021;27:1789-1796].

options.label: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: May be considered; similar to clinically positive nodal disease [ME-7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: If NED after local/regional therapy: consider adjuvant systemic therapy (category 2B). If NED after systemic therapy: observation. If residual resectable disease: consider further excision ± adjuvant systemic therapy.

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Options per MELSYS. For low-volume, combination immunotherapy toxicity may outweigh benefit [ME-8A].

options.label: Intralesional T-VEC (category 1), IL-2 (category 2B), RT (category 2B), local ablation
options.recommendation: T-VEC associated with 16% durable response rate in stage IIIB/IIIC [510].

options.label: Isolated limb infusion/perfusion (ILI/ILP) with melphalan
options.recommendation: For limb-only disease; center with expertise required [ME-8A].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After successful treatment: if NED after local/regional therapy, consider adjuvant systemic therapy (category 2B) or observation; if NED after systemic therapy, observation.

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred for therapy-naïve patients)
options.recommendation: Neoadjuvant approach should be considered depending on surgical morbidity [ME-17A]. Options per MELSYS.

options.label: Metastasectomy
options.recommendation: Resection if feasible and complete.

options.label: Stereotactic ablative RT (SBRT)
options.recommendation: For selected oligometastases.

options.label: T-VEC/intralesional therapy (for accessible lesions)
options.recommendation: Consider for cutaneous/subcutaneous lesions [ME-17].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After complete resection: adjuvant systemic therapy options include nivolumab (category 1), pembrolizumab (category 1), ipilimumab + nivolumab (preferred), or BRAF/MEK inhibitors (category 2B). Observation is also an option [ADJSYS 1, ME-17].

options.label: First-line systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Options: ipilimumab + nivolumab (category 1), nivolumab + relatlimab (category 1), anti-PD-1 monotherapy (category 1), or BRAF/MEK therapy if BRAF mutant (category 1). Choice based on patient characteristics and toxicity profile [MELSYS 1].

options.label: Palliative resection, RT, or T-VEC for symptomatic extracranial disease
options.recommendation: For symptom control [ME-17].

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: For poor performance status or after exhausting options.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for unresectable disease.

options.label: Multidisciplinary consultation (neurosurgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology)
options.recommendation: Initial treatment depends on symptoms, number/size of lesions, and extracranial burden [ME-J 1].

options.label: Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) or fractionated SRT for limited lesions; whole brain RT (WBRT) for palliative purposes if SRS not feasible
options.recommendation: SRS doses per RTOG 90-05: up to 24 Gy for ≤20 mm, up to 18 Gy for 21–30 mm, up to 15 Gy for 31–40 mm [ME-H 3]. Adjuvant WBRT after resection or SRS is not recommended [39].

options.label: Systemic therapy (consider as initial treatment for asymptomatic <3 cm brain mets not requiring steroids)
options.recommendation: Nivolumab/ipilimumab preferred due to high intracranial response. Anti-PD-1 monotherapy has modest activity. BRAF/MEK inhibitors show high intracranial response but shorter PFS [ME-J 3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: After resection of brain metastases, adjuvant SRS to cavity may be considered [Mahajan A, et al. Lancet Oncol 2017;18:1040-1048]. If NED, adjuvant systemic therapy options as for resected Stage IV.

nodes.criteria: True scar recurrence defined by in situ and/or radial growth phase abutting the scar

options.label: Re-excision to appropriate margins per T-stage of recurrence
options.recommendation: Consider lymphatic mapping/SLNB based on pathologic T-stage of recurrence [ME-13].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Follow guidelines for primary tumor of equivalent stage.

options.label: Complete excision to clear margins
options.recommendation: No need for wider margins; clear histologic margins sufficient [ME-14A].

options.label: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy
options.recommendation: May be favored; if excellent response, excision may not be necessary [ME-14].

options.label: T-VEC/intralesional therapy
options.recommendation: As per initial presentation.

adjuvant_logic.summary: After complete excision: systemic therapy (preferred) or observation. Consider adjuvant systemic therapy (category 2B for NED after local/regional therapy) [ADJSYS 1].

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Options per MELSYS.

options.label: Intralesional T-VEC (category 1), IL-2 (category 2B), RT (category 2B)
options.recommendation: As per initial unresectable in-transit.

options.label: ILI/ILP with melphalan
options.recommendation: For limb-only recurrence.

adjuvant_logic.summary: If NED after therapy: systemic therapy or observation.

nodes.criteria: Resectable nodal recurrence, no prior LND or incomplete LND

options.label: Excision of recurrence + TLND (if incomplete/no prior LND) or neoadjuvant systemic therapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Neoadjuvant approach is preferred given EFS benefit [ME-16].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy (category 1 for resectable nodal recurrence) and/or RT to nodal basin (category 2B) for high risk. If prior anti-PD-1 exposure, ipilimumab may be an option [ADJSYS 1, ME-16].

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: Per MELSYS.

options.label: Palliative RT
options.recommendation: For symptom control.

options.label: Intralesional T-VEC
options.recommendation: For accessible lesions.

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant; treat as metastatic.

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred for therapy-naïve)
options.recommendation: Options per MELSYS.

options.label: Metastasectomy or SBRT
options.recommendation: Consider after systemic therapy or for isolated lesions.

adjuvant_logic.summary: After complete resection: adjuvant systemic therapy options as for de novo Stage IV oligometastatic. Selection informed by prior therapy [ME-17].

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred)
options.recommendation: First-line options as per MELSYS 1. For progression on anti-PD-1, consider ipilimumab combination or BRAF/MEK if BRAF mutant.

options.label: Palliative resection/RT/T-VEC for symptomatic disease
options.recommendation: For symptom control.

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant; continue treatment.

items.text: Complete history and physical examination with attention to locoregional area, draining lymph nodes, and complete skin exam
items.recommendation: Recommended for all stages at initial presentation and follow-up [ME-1].

items.text: Assessment of melanoma-related risk factors: male sex, age >50 years, phenotypic predisposition (atypical nevi, increased mole count, sun sensitivity, red hair/blue eyes), personal history of prior melanoma or non-melanoma skin cancers, immunosuppression (organ transplant, HIV, hematologic malignancies), family history of melanoma or pancreatic cancer, environmental UV exposure (tanning bed use, sun exposure) [ME-A 1 of 2]
items.recommendation: Should be assessed at initial presentation and updated periodically.

items.text: Minimal elements: Breslow thickness (to nearest 0.1 mm), ulceration status (present or absent), dermal mitotic rate (#/mm2), deep and peripheral margin status, microsatellitosis (present or absent), pure desmoplasia if present, lymphovascular/angiolymphatic invasion, neurotropism/perineural invasion [ME-B 2 of 3]
items.recommendation: Mandatory for all biopsy and wide excision specimens.

items.text: Consider molecular testing (CGH, FISH, GEP) for histologically equivocal lesions. BRAF testing recommended only if needed for adjuvant or systemic therapy decisions. MGPT (multigene panel testing) may be considered for Stage IV or recurrence [ME-C 1 of 8–5 of 8]
items.recommendation: Not routine for primary diagnosis unless indicated.

items.text: Stage 0, IA, IB, II: Routine cross-sectional imaging not recommended unless needed for surgical planning or prior to adjuvant therapy (Stage IIB/IIC). Nodal basin US not a substitute for SLNB [ME-D 2 of 5]
items.recommendation: Imaging only to evaluate specific signs or symptoms.

items.text: Stage IIIA (sentinel node positive): Consider cross-sectional imaging for baseline staging or prior to adjuvant therapy [ME-D 2 of 5]

Stage IIIB/C/D: Perform cross-sectional imaging with or without brain imaging for baseline staging. Baseline risk of metastatic disease in clinically node-positive patients is 11.6% overall; 1.8% brain metastases [Boutros CS, et al. J Natl Cancer Inst 2025;117:180-187]
items.recommendation: Whole-body and brain imaging standard for clinically node-positive patients.

items.text: Stage IV: Perform cross-sectional and brain imaging (MRI preferred) [ME-D 2 of 5]
Recurrence (local satellite/in-transit, nodal, distant): Cross-sectional imaging with or without brain imaging to assess extent of disease [ME-D 2 of 5]
Routine blood tests not recommended for Stage 0–II. Serum LDH recommended at diagnosis of Stage IV disease [ME-9]
items.recommendation: LDH is prognostic in Stage IV.

items.text: BRAF mutation testing: recommended for Stage III at high risk for recurrence and for Stage IV to guide targeted therapy. KIT testing for appropriate clinical settings (mucosal, acral, chronic sun-damaged skin). MGPT if feasible [ME-C 3 of 8]
items.recommendation: Tissue from metastasis preferred; if not available, ctDNA testing can be considered but negative ctDNA should prompt tissue testing.

items.text: SLNB should be discussed with all clinical Stage IB or II melanoma. For T1a (<0.8 mm, no ulceration) with risk <5%, SLNB not recommended. For T1b or T1a with adverse features (risk 5–10%), discuss and consider. For Stage IB (T2a) and II (T2b+), discuss and offer [ME-F 2 of 5]
items.recommendation: Decision based on risk assessment using thickness, ulceration, mitotic rate, age, location, LVI. Predictive GEP tests (CP-GEP) may support shared decision-making in select T1b/T2a patients when patient would decline SLNB if risk <10% [Hieken TJ, et al. JAMA Surg 2025;160:1358-1366].

items.text: Consider SLNB for resectable in-transit disease if it will affect decision for adjuvant therapy (category 2B) [ME-F 2 of 5]

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary curative modality for primary cutaneous melanoma and regional nodal disease. It is also used for isolated recurrences and oligometastatic disease. Wide excision of the primary tumor with appropriate margins achieves local control, while SLNB provides staging. For clinically positive nodes, therapeutic lymph node dissection (TLND) is performed, often after neoadjuvant systemic therapy. In the sentinel node-positive setting, completion lymph node dissection (CLND) is no longer routine due to lack of MSS/OS benefit [336,452].
principles: Wide excision margins are based on Breslow thickness: in situ 0.5–1 cm; ≤1.0 mm 1 cm (category 1); >1.0–2.0 mm 1–2 cm (category 1); >2.0–4.0 mm 2 cm (category 1); >4.0 mm 2 cm (category 1) [ME-E 1 of 3].

Depth of excision: to subcutaneous fat for MIS; to fascia for invasive melanoma (fascia preserved unless involved). Narrower margins may be modified for anatomic/functional considerations but increase risk of margin positivity and local recurrence [ME-E 1 of 3].

SLNB should be performed at the time of wide excision for clinically node-negative patients with appropriate risk. Lymphatic mapping with blue dye and radiotracer. Sentinel nodes evaluated with serial sectioning and IHC [ME-F 3 of 4].

CLND is not routinely recommended for sentinel node-positive patients. Active nodal ultrasound surveillance is preferred. In select cases (e.g., high risk of additional positive nodes, inability to adhere to surveillance), CLND may be discussed [ME-5A].

TLND should be anatomically complete: axillary dissection levels I–III; inguinofemoral dissection for groin; superficial parotidectomy + neck dissection for head/neck with parotid involvement. Pelvic LND considered for positive Cloquet's node, ≥3 positive inguinofemoral nodes, or imaging evidence [ME-G].

Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is preferred for clinically positive nodal disease to improve EFS and potentially reduce extent of surgery. Index lymph node marking may allow targeted resection [NEOSYS 2].

For melanomas on face, ears, acral sites, Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) may be considered for T1a lesions with appropriate immunostaining and permanent section analysis of debulking specimen [ME-E 1 of 3].
Digit-sparing surgery (wide excision or MMS) may be an option for subungual MIS and select thin tumors (<0.8 mm) [15].

procedures.name: Wide Excision of Primary Melanoma
procedures.approach: Elliptical excision with margins as above, oriented longitudinally on extremities, parallel to lymphatics. Closure may require skin graft or flap if complex, but should be delayed until margin assessment complete.
procedures.key_complications: Wound dehiscence, infection, seroma, hypertrophic scar, functional/cosmetic deformity

procedures.name: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (SLNB)
procedures.approach: Preoperative lymphoscintigraphy with Tc-99m radiotracer; intraoperative blue dye and gamma probe. Excision of all sentinel nodes (identified by blue color, >10% of highest ex vivo count).
procedures.indications: Clinical Stage IB–II with ≥5–10% risk of positivity; selected Stage III in-transit or microsatellites
procedures.key_complications: Lymphedema (low risk <5%), wound infection, allergic reaction to blue dye, nerve injury [216–218]

procedures.name: Completion Lymph Node Dissection (CLND)
procedures.approach: Anatomically complete dissection of the involved nodal basin. For axilla: levels I–III; for groin: inguinofemoral ± pelvic; for neck: neck dissection levels I–V; for parotid: superficial parotidectomy.
procedures.indications: Select sentinel node-positive patients (high risk of non-SLN positivity, inability to adhere to surveillance, regional control needed). Also for incomplete prior LND in recurrence setting
procedures.key_complications: Lymphedema (20–30%), wound infection, seroma, nerve injury, decreased range of motion [330–333]

procedures.name: Therapeutic Lymph Node Dissection (TLND)
procedures.approach: Same anatomic boundaries as CLND, performed for clinically positive nodes. Increasingly performed after neoadjuvant therapy. Index lymph node resection may be substituted in responders.
procedures.indications: Clinically positive nodes (with or without neoadjuvant therapy)
procedures.key_complications: Lymphedema (particularly groin), wound complications, nerve injury, lymphedema [378]

procedures.name: Mohs Micrographic Surgery (MMS)
procedures.approach: Tissue-sparing excision with frozen section margin control using melanocytic IHC stains (MART-1). Central debulking for permanent sections.
procedures.indications: Select T1a melanomas in anatomically constrained areas (face, ears, acral); also for MIS/lentigo maligna with comprehensive margin assessment
procedures.key_complications: Risk of incomplete staging if not combined with permanent sections; local recurrence low when performed correctly [13]

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is defined as the ability to achieve complete (R0) surgical removal with acceptable morbidity. For primary melanoma, resectability is generally always feasible. For nodal disease, resectability is determined by absence of major neurovascular involvement and ability to achieve clear margins. For oligometastatic disease, resectability is considered if all disease can be removed with negative margins. Borderline resectable refers to disease where upfront surgery would be morbid; neoadjuvant therapy may convert to resectable. Unresectable disease includes involvement of major neurovascular structures, distant nodal disease where surgery alone has minimal benefit, or widely disseminated disease [ME-6A, ME-G].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) has several roles in melanoma management: definitive therapy for lentigo maligna (LM) when surgery is not feasible; adjuvant therapy for high-risk primary tumors (desmoplastic melanoma, extensive neurotropism, positive margins) and for nodal basins at high risk of recurrence; palliative therapy for symptomatic metastases; and definitive/ablative therapy for brain metastases (SRS/SRT) and extracranial oligometastases (SBRT). RT is not routinely used as adjuvant for primary melanoma with clear margins.
principles: Techniques: external beam RT (EBRT) using modern modalities (IMRT, IGRT) to reduce toxicity. Hypofractionation is commonly used for melanoma.
Adjuvant nodal RT reduces lymph node field recurrence but does not improve RFS or OS; benefits must be weighed against toxicities (lymphedema) [410].
Adjuvant brain RT (WBRT) after resection or SRS is not recommended due to lack of OS benefit and cognitive decline risk [34,39].

SRS is preferred radiation modality for brain metastases; single-fraction doses based on RTOG 90-05: 24 Gy for ≤20 mm, 18 Gy for 21–30 mm, 15 Gy for 31–40 mm [31]. Fractionated SRT for larger lesions: 24–35 Gy in 3–5 fractions.
Concurrent BRAF/MEK inhibitors with RT increases toxicity; hold BRAF/MEK inhibitors ≥3 days before and after fractionated RT and ≥1 day before and after SRS [52,749].
Immune checkpoint inhibitors may be safely combined with RT; no clear evidence of increased toxicity [48,49].

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT for Lentigo Maligna
dose_frameworks.indication: Medically inoperable LM or when surgery not feasible [ME-H 1]

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT for High-Risk Primary
dose_frameworks.indication: Desmoplastic melanoma with risk factors (T4, head/neck, neurotropism, positive margins) [ME-H 1]

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant Nodal Basin RT (Standard Fractionation)
dose_frameworks.indication: High risk of nodal recurrence (extracapsular extension, multiple positive nodes, large nodes) [ME-H 2]

dose_frameworks.name: SRS for Brain Metastases – Single Fraction
dose_frameworks.indication: Small tumors (≤40 mm): 24 Gy (≤20 mm), 18 Gy (21–30 mm), 15 Gy (31–40 mm) [ME-H 3]

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative WBRT
dose_frameworks.indication: When SRS/SRT not feasible; consider hippocampal avoidance + memantine for better prognosis [ME-H 3]

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT for Extracranial Metastases
dose_frameworks.indication: Bone pain, compression symptoms; higher doses/larger fractions for durable palliation [ME-H 4]

approaches.name: Adjuvant Nodal Basin RT
approaches.indication: Patients at high risk for nodal recurrence after TLND: non-response or non-receipt of neoadjuvant therapy, extracapsular extension, location, number/size of nodes (category 2B) [ME-H 2]
approaches.key_trial: ANZMTG 01.02/TROG 02.01: reduced lymph node field recurrence (HR 0.54) but no RFS or OS benefit [410]
approaches.toxicities: Lymphedema (limb), skin fibrosis, pain, nerve damage, joint stiffness [410]

approaches.name: SRS for Brain Metastases
approaches.dose_fractionation: Single fraction 15–24 Gy per RTOG 90-05 or fractionated SRT 24–35 Gy/3–5 fractions
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: BRAF/MEK inhibitors should be held; immune checkpoint inhibitors safe [52,49]
approaches.indication: Primary treatment for brain metastases; adjuvant to resection cavity (12–20 Gy per cavity volume) [ME-H 3]
approaches.key_trial: NCCTG N107C/CEC.3: Adjuvant SRS vs WBRT after resection; SRS associated with less cognitive decline, no OS difference [34]
approaches.toxicities: Radionecrosis (risk increased with lesion size), edema, headache, seizures

approaches.name: Palliative RT for Symptomatic Metastases
approaches.dose_fractionation: 8 Gy/1 fraction, 20 Gy/5 fractions, 30 Gy/10 fractions, or 40 Gy/8 fractions
approaches.indication: Symptomatic extracranial metastases (bone pain, bleeding, obstruction) [ME-H 4]
approaches.key_trial: Overgaard et al. showed 60% clinical regression with hypofractionated RT [705]

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for cutaneous melanoma has evolved dramatically since the early 2010s. For unresectable Stage III/IV, immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1 alone or combined with ipilimumab) and BRAF/MEK inhibitors (for BRAF V600-mutant) are the mainstays, offering long-term remissions in ~50% of patients [35]. In the adjuvant setting, anti-PD-1 agents and dabrafenib/trametinib improve RFS and are FDA-approved for Stage III and Stage IIB/IIC. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is preferred for clinically positive Stage III nodes, with ipilimumab + nivolumab showing highest pathologic response rates. For second-line or subsequent therapy, options include switching class (e.g., from immunotherapy to targeted therapy or vice versa), TIL therapy (lifileucel) for selected patients, and less effective cytotoxic agents. Subcutaneous formulations of nivolumab, pembrolizumab, and atezolizumab are available [SYSCON 2].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant – Stage IIB/IIC (pathologic, SLNB strongly recommended)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Reduced relapse events vs. observation; longer follow-up needed for OS impact [ADJSYS 1, KEYNOTE-716, CheckMate 76K]. Shared decision-making regarding risk/benefit.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant – Stage III (sentinel node positive, microscopic satellites, clinically positive nodes, CSIT)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (if BRAF V600+, category 1 for clinically positive nodes), Nivolumab (category 1 for CSIT limited resectable), Pembrolizumab
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: All agents improve RFS vs. placebo or ipilimumab. COMBI-AD, KEYNOTE-054, CheckMate 238 trials. BRAF/MEK preferred for BRAF+ due to oral route and different toxicity profile. For Stage IIIA with low tumor burden (<0.3 mm), observation often preferred [ADJSYS 2].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ipilimumab (useful in certain circumstances for patients with prior anti-PD-1 therapy); observation

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant – Local satellite/in-transit recurrence (NED after local therapy)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (if BRAF+), Nivolumab, Pembrolizumab (all category 2B for NED after local/regional therapy)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Extrapolation from Stage III adjuvant trials. Systemic therapy options category 2B due to less direct evidence.
Based on COMBI-AD, CheckMate 238, KEYNOTE-054. For prior anti-PD-1 exposure, ipilimumab may be an option.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ipilimumab (useful in certain circumstances if prior anti-PD-1); observation

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: IMMUNED trial showed ipilimumab + nivolumab improved RFS in resected Stage IV [ADJSYS 2]. BRAF/MEK options category 2B.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Dabrafenib/Trametinib, Encorafenib/Binimetinib, Vemurafenib/Cobimetinib (all category 2B if BRAF+); Ipilimumab (useful in certain circumstances if prior anti-PD-1)

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (category 1), Nivolumab + Relatlimab (category 1), Nivolumab (category 1), Pembrolizumab (category 1)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Combination checkpoint blockade preferred for higher response rate, PFS, and OS, albeit with higher toxicity. Anti-PD-1 monotherapy appropriate for patients with lower risk of irAEs. BRAF/MEK therapy (category 1) is alternative for BRAF+ but first-line immunotherapy shows superiority [DREAMseq trial].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (BRAF+), Encorafenib/Binimetinib (BRAF+), Vemurafenib/Cobimetinib (BRAF+); Low-dose Ipilimumab + Pembrolizumab (category 2B)

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Combination checkpoint blockade (if not used first-line), anti-PD-1 monotherapy, BRAF/MEK therapy (if BRAF+), TIL therapy (lifileucel)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: For progression on anti-PD-1: ipilimumab + nivolumab shows 25–33% response rates. Nivolumab/relatlimab shows 9–12% ORR [MELSYS 3]. TIL therapy for good PS patients after anti-PD-1 and BRAF/MEK.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ipilimumab monotherapy; KIT inhibitors (imatinib) for KIT+; NTRK/ROS1 inhibitors for rare fusions; Cytotoxic agents (temozolomide, carboplatin/paclitaxel); Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab; T-VEC + Ipilimumab (category 2B)

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (Combination Checkpoint Blockade)
key_regimens.setting: First-line metastatic/unresectable; neoadjuvant for Stage III

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.dose: 3 mg/kg (alternatively 1 mg/kg if ipilimumab 3 mg/kg used)
drugs.day: Same day every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then nivolumab monotherapy 240 mg Q2W or 480 mg Q4W

key_regimens.cycle_days: Induction: 4 cycles of 3 weeks. Maintenance: Q2W or Q4W.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3–4 irAEs in 54–59%: colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, myocarditis. Fatal AEs rare but include colitis and myocarditis [645].

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab + Relatlimab-rmbw (Fixed-Dose Combination)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Intermediate toxicity between anti-PD-1 alone and ipilimumab combination; LAG-3-related AEs.

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab Monotherapy
key_regimens.setting: Adjuvant Stage IIB–IV; First-line and subsequent metastatic; Neoadjuvant

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg (or 2 mg/kg for 10 mg/kg in trials) Q3W; alternatively 400 mg Q6W
drugs.route: IV (subcutaneous formulation: pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph available)
drugs.day: Day 1 every 3 weeks (or every 6 weeks flat dose)

key_regimens.cycle_days: 21-day cycles for 12–24 months (adjuvant) or until progression (metastatic)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3–4 AEs ~14–17%: fatigue, rash, pruritus, diarrhea, colitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies. Immune-related AEs may be lifelong (15% risk of endocrine dysfunction) [ADJSYS 2].

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab Monotherapy
key_regimens.setting: Adjuvant Stage IIB–IV; First-line and subsequent metastatic; Neoadjuvant

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.dose: 240 mg Q2W or 480 mg Q4W (adjuvant: 3 mg/kg Q2W in trial)
drugs.route: IV (subcutaneous formulation: nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy available)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3–4 AEs ~22%: similar to pembrolizumab; CheckMate 066: 15% grade 3–4 related AEs [590].

key_regimens.name: Dabrafenib + Trametinib (BRAF/MEK Combination)
key_regimens.setting: Adjuvant Stage III (BRAF V600+); First-line and subsequent metastatic; Neoadjuvant
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Pyrexia (55%), chills, fatigue, nausea, diarrhea, arthralgia, headache. Grade 3–4: pyrexia (7%), hypertension (14%), elevated ALT/AST (3–9%). Cutaneous SCC less common than with BRAF monotherapy [448].

key_regimens.name: Encorafenib + Binimetinib (BRAF/MEK Combination)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3–4: CPK elevation (12%), hypertension (6%), ALT elevation (5%). Less pyrexia than dabrafenib/trametinib? [665]

key_regimens.name: Vemurafenib + Cobimetinib (BRAF/MEK Combination)

drugs.name: Cobimetinib
drugs.day: Once daily for 21 days on, 7 days off

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3–4: GGT elevation (15%), lipase elevation (3%), photosensitivity, rash, diarrhea. Increased risk of serous retinopathy [677].

key_regimens.name: TIL Therapy (Lifileucel)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for metastatic disease after anti-PD-1 and BRAF/MEK if applicable

drugs.name: Lifileucel (autologous TIL)
drugs.day: After nonmyeloablative chemotherapy and followed by high-dose IL-2

key_regimens.cycle_days: Single treatment; requires prior chemotherapy preparative regimen (cyclophosphamide + fludarabine).
key_regimens.key_toxicities: High-dose IL-2 toxicities (capillary leak syndrome, hypotension, fever, chills, cardiac, renal). Myelosuppression from preparative chemotherapy. Contraindicated in inadequate organ function, poor PS, untreated brain metastases [MELSYS 3].

key_regimens.name: Talimogene Laherparepvec (T-VEC) Intralesional
key_regimens.setting: Unresectable Stage III/IV with injectable lesions; first-line or subsequent

drugs.name: T-VEC
drugs.dose: Initial dose: 10^6 PFU/mL, then subsequent doses 10^8 PFU/mL
drugs.day: Every 2 weeks for at least 6 doses

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue (50%), chills, pyrexia, nausea, injection-site pain. Grade 3–4: 11% [510].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Principles of Treatment Response Assessment
treatment_response_assessment.timing: For patients receiving neoadjuvant therapy, cross-sectional imaging is recommended after 6 to 12 weeks to exclude residual or metastatic disease and assess for surgical planning. For active systemic therapy (other than complete surgical resection), imaging throughout treatment at clinically appropriate intervals (every 2–6 months) is recommended for Stage III in-transit disease, unresectable nodal disease, limited resectable distant disease, and disseminated disease. For patients rendered NED by surgery, follow-up imaging recommendations are stage-specific (see follow-up guidelines).
response_logic: Radiographic or clinically evident increase in tumor size may precede regression early in the course of immune-based therapy (pseudoprogression). Average time to response ranges from 6 to 12 weeks for most asymptomatic patients. It is reasonable to continue immunotherapy beyond progression for an additional interval of 6 to 10 weeks with short-interval imaging. Continued growth 16 weeks after starting immunotherapy should be considered true progression [SYSCON 1].
For BRAF-targeted therapy, median time to response is approximately 1.5 months. Responses are relatively short-lived, with median duration ~5–10 months [151,463].

Active nodal basin surveillance for sentinel node-positive patients who did not undergo CLND: clinical exam and ultrasound/imaging every 4 months for first 2 years, then every 6 months for years 3–5 (consistent with MSLT-II and DeCOG protocols) [ME-5A].
imaging_recommendations: Cross-sectional imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast, ± neck CT/MRI) and/or whole-body FDG-PET/CT are used. Brain MRI with and without IV contrast is preferred for CNS imaging [ME-D 1].

For stage IIB–IV NED after treatment, consider imaging every 3–12 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for another 3 years (category 2B). More frequent brain MRI recommended for patients with prior brain metastases. Routine imaging after 3–5 years not recommended [ME-11].
Nodal basin ultrasound is recommended for post-SLNB surveillance (if CLND not performed), where expertise available. It is preferred over cross-sectional imaging for nodal assessment [ME-D 4].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Repeat biopsy is essential for confirming recurrence when clinically indicated. Tissue is always preferred over cytology for molecular testing; obtain tissue to ascertain BRAF, KIT, and consider MGPT [ME-9].

For true scar recurrence (persistent disease), biopsy to confirm before re-excision. For local satellite/in-transit recurrence, biopsy to confirm and differentiate from scar recurrence [ME-14, ME-13].

Salvage surgery should be considered for oligometastatic or isolated nodal recurrence after multidisciplinary discussion. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is preferred for resectable nodal recurrence [ME-16].

For patients who progress on systemic therapy, selection of subsequent therapy should be informed by prior response: if progression during/shortly after prior therapy, consider agents of a different class; if disease control with relapse >3 months after discontinuation, reinduction with same class may be considered [ME-7A].

prognosis.overall: Outcome for cutaneous melanoma depends on stage at presentation. For localized disease with primary tumors ≤1.0 mm, 5-year and 10-year disease-specific survival (DSS) exceeds 96%. For localized melanomas >1.0 mm, 5- and 10-year DSS range from 75% to 94% depending on thickness, ulceration, and mitotic rate. When regional nodes are involved, 5- and 10-year DSS range from 24% to 93% based on extent of nodal involvement, though these survival rates exclude data following use of novel targeted and immune therapies. Patients with clinically occult nodal disease detected by sentinel lymph node biopsy generally have better survival than those with clinically evident nodal disease [28-30]. Historically, long-term survival in distant metastatic melanoma was <10% at 5 years [31-34], but effective systemic therapies have made long-term remission possible for approximately 50% of patients [35].

by_stage.context: Localized disease, primary tumor ≤1.0 mm thickness, no ulceration.
Localized melanomas >1.0 mm, depending on thickness, ulceration, and mitotic rate [28].
Depends on extent of nodal involvement; clinically occult (sentinel node detected) have better survival than clinically evident nodal disease [28-30].
Long-term survival <10% at 5 years prior to targeted/immunotherapy; median survival <1 year [31-34]. With effective systemic therapies, long-term remission possible in ~50% [35].

prognostic_factors: Regional lymph node status (number of positive nodes, tumor burden, clinical vs. occult)
Site of distant metastases (M1a skin/soft tissue, M1b lung, M1c other visceral, M1d CNS)
Sentinel lymph node status is the strongest predictor of survival for clinical stage I/II
prognosis.prognostic_context: AJCC staging uses T, N, M categories to define prognostic stage groups. Key primary tumor factors are Breslow thickness (reported to nearest 0.1 mm) and ulceration (present/absent). Dermal mitotic rate remains an important prognostic factor across all thickness categories [85-92]. For stage III, clinical nodal status (nonpalpable vs. palpable) and number of metastatic nodes are most important [129,130]. For stage IV, site of metastases and LDH are prognostic [129,144,145].

disease_related.management: Re-excision to appropriate margins; consider sentinel lymph node biopsy if not previously performed; adjuvant radiation or systemic therapy based on stage.
Complete excision if limited; intralesional injection (T-VEC, IL-2); isolated limb infusion/perfusion; systemic therapy; clinical trial.
Completion lymph node dissection (if not done); excision; adjuvant systemic therapy; consider adjuvant nodal basin RT in selected high-risk patients (category 2B).
Systemic therapy (immunotherapy, targeted therapy); palliative RT; surgical resection for oligometastases; best supportive care.

Multidisciplinary evaluation; stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) or fractionated SRT; surgery for large/symptomatic lesions; systemic therapy with intracranial activity (nivolumab/ipilimumab, BRAF/MEK inhibitors); corticosteroids for symptoms; whole brain RT for palliation when SRS not feasible.

immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) affecting skin (rash, pruritus, vitiligo), gastrointestinal (diarrhea/colitis), hepatic (elevated ALT/AST, hepatitis), endocrine (hypophysitis, adrenal insufficiency, thyroid dysfunction), pulmonary (pneumonitis), renal (nephritis), and neurologic systems
Severe/fatal irAEs: colitis, myocarditis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, neurotoxic effects [646]
targeted_therapy: Pyrexia (common with BRAF/MEK inhibitors, ~55% with dabrafenib/trametinib; episodic, median duration 9 days, onset 2-4 weeks) [ME-I]
Cutaneous toxicities: squamous cell carcinoma, keratoacanthoma, photosensitivity, rash, palmoplantar hyperkeratosis (more common with BRAF inhibitor monotherapy)
Cardiovascular: hypertension, LVEF decline, QT prolongation
surgery: Functional deficits (e.g., after extensive head/neck or limb surgery)
radiation: Acute dermatitis (especially with concurrent BRAF inhibitor therapy [735])

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care in melanoma patients should be integrated throughout treatment and survivorship. Key areas include management of treatment-related toxicities, nutritional support, antiemetic protocols, growth factor support, VTE prophylaxis, pain management, psychosocial support, and dental care. The NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities provide detailed management algorithms. Best supportive care should be considered for patients with poor performance status or those who progress despite multiple lines of therapy.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Routine nutritional screening is recommended. For patients receiving systemic therapy with significant gastrointestinal toxicities (colitis, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea), dietary modifications, oral supplements, and involvement of a registered dietitian may be necessary. For severe colitis on immune checkpoint inhibitors, corticosteroids and supportive care are indicated.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Antiemetic prophylaxis should be based on emetogenic potential of chemotherapy agents. For highly emetogenic regimens (e.g., cisplatin-based), recommend NK1 receptor antagonist + 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone. For moderately emetogenic (e.g., carboplatin/paclitaxel), 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone. For immunotherapy, antiemetics as needed for mild nausea; corticosteroids may be used for irAE management.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: G-CSF prophylaxis is not routinely indicated for melanoma chemotherapy regimens except in high-risk patients. For cytotoxic regimens like carboplatin/paclitaxel, primary prophylaxis may be considered if febrile neutropenia risk >20% or based on patient factors. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Myeloid Growth Factors.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: VTE risk in melanoma patients is elevated due to malignancy and potential surgery. For hospitalized patients, pharmacologic prophylaxis recommended. For ambulatory patients receiving systemic therapy, consider VTE prophylaxis if high risk (Khorana score ≥2). Inpatient surgical patients should receive prophylaxis; early mobilization encouraged.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain should be assessed regularly using validated scales. For mild pain, acetaminophen/NSAIDs (with caution for renal or GI issues). For moderate-severe pain, opioids as per WHO ladder. Neuropathic pain from surgery or chemotherapy may benefit from gabapentinoids or tricyclic antidepressants. Palliative RT for bone metastases. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Patients with melanoma have significant psychosocial needs including anxiety, depression, body image concerns, and fear of recurrence. Routine screening with validated tools (PHQ-9, GAD-7) is recommended. Offer counseling, support groups, and psychiatric referral as needed. Early structured psychiatric intervention may improve survival outcomes [782]. Patient education on sun safety and self-examination is crucial.
supportive_care.dental_care: Dental evaluation is recommended before initiating therapy that may cause mucositis or immunosuppression. For patients receiving immune checkpoint inhibitors, dental procedures should be considered if needed. Patients on BRAF/MEK inhibitors with mucositis may benefit from oral hygiene protocols and topical anesthetics.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Follow-up schedules are based on stage and risk of recurrence. For stage 0 in situ: annual skin exam for life. For stage IA–IIA (NED): H&P every 6–12 months for 5 years, then annually. For stage IIB–IV (NED): H&P every 3–6 months for 2 years, every 3–12 months for 3 years, then annually. Lifetime dermatologic surveillance is appropriate due to increased risk of subsequent melanomas [63,757,760,777]. Common recommendations include complete skin exam, patient education on self-examination, sun protection, and genetic counseling referral for >2 invasive melanomas or family history patterns [ME-12].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Rationale includes early detection of recurrence (which may be more amenable to treatment) and identification of second primary melanomas. Most recurrences are detected by patient or physician exam [756-762]. Imaging-detected recurrences are more likely distant. The impact of surveillance on overall survival is not proven in prospective trials, but early detection of asymptomatic recurrence may improve outcomes with effective therapies [17]. Follow-up should be individualized based on risk.
late_effects_screening: Lymphedema: Assess for extremity swelling after lymph node dissection; refer to certified lymphedema therapist; consider compressive garments, massage, and exercise. Referral for lymphovascular revascularization for refractory cases.

Endocrine dysfunction: Monitor for hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, hypophysitis in patients treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors; approximately 15% risk of lifelong endocrine dysfunction. Screening labs as clinically indicated.
Neurocognitive effects: Patients who received whole brain radiotherapy should be monitored for cognitive decline; consider memantine during/after WBRT [38].
Second primary cancers: Skin cancer screening; consider cancer surveillance based on germline findings (CDKN2A, BAP1, etc.).
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence risk is stage-dependent. For stage I–II, recurrences are ~15–20% local/in-transit, ~50% regional nodes, ~29% distant. For stage III, ~50% are distant. Median time to recurrence is shorter with higher stage. Over 95% of recurrences occur within 3 years for stage IIIA/B, and within 2 years for stage IIIC [183]. Late recurrence (>10 years) can occur, especially for early-stage [774-776].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Stage 0 in situ: annual skin exam for life.
Stage IA–IIA NED: H&P every 6–12 months for 5 years, then annually. Routine blood tests not recommended. Routine imaging not recommended.

Stage IIB–IV NED: H&P every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 3–12 months for 3 years, then annually. Routine blood tests not recommended unless for post-treatment monitoring. Consider imaging every 3–12 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for another 3 years (category 2B). Routine imaging not recommended after 3–5 years.

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast and/or FDG-PET/CT may be used for surveillance in high-risk patients. Brain MRI with/without contrast preferred for CNS imaging; consider periodic brain MRI for patients with prior brain metastases or high-risk stage IIIB or higher without CNS mets [ME-D].
imaging_strategy: Nodal basin ultrasound: Preferred surveillance for patients with positive SLNB who did not undergo CLND; frequency every 4 months for first 2 years, then every 6 months for years 3–5 (consistent with MSLT-II and DeCOG trials).

Cross-sectional imaging (CT or FDG-PET/CT): For stage IIB–IV, consider every 3–12 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for 3 years (category 2B). Choice depends on clinical circumstances; FDG-PET/CT may be more sensitive for distant metastases.
Brain MRI: For patients with prior brain metastases, more frequent surveillance recommended. Periodic brain MRI up to 3 years may be appropriate in stage IIIB or higher without prior CNS metastases.
Imaging is not recommended for stage 0, IA, IIA NED. Chest x-ray has non-uniform application; cross-sectional imaging preferred.
Treatment response assessment: For active systemic therapy, imaging every 2–6 months as clinically indicated.
laboratory_monitoring: Routine blood tests are not recommended for surveillance in asymptomatic patients.
LDH may be obtained at diagnosis of stage IV disease for prognostic value.
Role of circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) for surveillance is still being defined; not routinely recommended.
For patients on immune checkpoint inhibitors, monitor thyroid function, liver enzymes, and creatinine periodically as clinically indicated.
supportive_follow_up: Patient education on regular skin and lymph node self-examination.
Sun safety counseling: avoid peak hours, protective clothing, broad-spectrum sunscreen.
Genetic counseling referral for p16/CDKN2A testing if >2 invasive melanomas or family history patterns.
Referral for lymphedema management and psychosocial support as needed.
Coordination of care across specialties for annual skin exam to avoid duplication.

special_situations.situation: Stage III melanoma with microsatellites only (no satellite, in-transit, or nodal disease)
special_situations.key_message: These patients demonstrate more favorable survival compared with those with a positive SLNB [Bartlett EK. J Surg Oncol 2019;119:200-207; Karakousis GC, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2019;26:33-41]. Adjuvant therapy trials may not be applicable to this subgroup.
special_situations.management: Observation or adjuvant systemic therapy based on risk discussion. BRAF testing recommended if considering targeted therapy.
special_situations.evidence: Patients who were microsatellite-positive but SLN-negative were not studied in adjuvant therapy trials [ME-4A].

special_situations.key_message: SLNB positivity is less common compared to mixed desmoplastic and conventional subtypes. Role of SLNB remains controversial. Adjuvant RT may be considered for high-risk local recurrence factors.
special_situations.management: Multidisciplinary consultation including dermatopathologist. Consider forgoing SLNB if confirmed pure desmoplastic. Wide excision with clear margins; consider adjuvant RT for T4 primary, neurotropism, close margins, or anatomic sites where re-resection challenging (category 2B).
special_situations.evidence: Variability in SLN positivity rates due to lack of standardized criteria [ME-1A, ME-3A]. Adjuvant RT improves local control in retrospective series [401-404].

special_situations.situation: Melanoma of unknown primary (in-transit, nodal, or distant metastatic)
special_situations.key_message: Dermatologic exam is warranted to assess for primary cutaneous melanoma. Ocular and mucosal examinations are not necessary unless symptomatic.
special_situations.management: Treat as appropriate stage (III or IV) with standard workup and treatment pathways. Evaluate for BRAF and KIT mutations. Consider germline testing.

special_situations.key_message: The high risk of toxicities associated with combination immunotherapy (nivolumab/ipilimumab or nivolumab/relatlimab) may outweigh benefit.
special_situations.management: Consider local therapy options (T-VEC, intralesional IL-2, excision) or single-agent anti-PD-1. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy may be used but weigh toxicity.

special_situations.situation: Patients who progress on anti-PD-1 monotherapy
special_situations.key_message: Second-line options depend on prior therapy and mutation status. Combination checkpoint blockade (ipilimumab/anti-PD-1) produces ~25-33% response rates [SYSCON 1 of 2]. Nivolumab/relatlimab produces ~10-12% response.
special_situations.management: If BRAF V600 positive, consider BRAF/MEK inhibitors. Consider TIL therapy (lifileucel) for good performance status. Other options: clinical trial, T-VEC, lenvatinib/pembrolizumab, cytotoxic chemotherapy, best supportive care.
special_situations.evidence: Based on prospective trials and NCCN consensus [MELSYS 3 of 10].

special_situations.situation: Brain metastases in patients not requiring corticosteroids
special_situations.key_message: Systemic therapy as sole initial treatment may be considered in select patients with low-volume asymptomatic brain metastases.
special_situations.management: Nivolumab/ipilimumab combination preferred due to superior intracranial activity. Close surveillance with brain MRI every 6-8 weeks. BRAF/MEK inhibitors for BRAF-mutant if symptomatic or high burden.
special_situations.evidence: CheckMate 204: nivolumab/ipilimumab in asymptomatic brain metastases showed intracranial response rate 50-57% [616]. COMBI-MB: dabrafenib/trametinib intracranial response 44-58% [676].

special_situations.key_message: Melanoma is one of the most common cancers diagnosed during pregnancy. Multidisciplinary management is essential.
special_situations.management: Treatment should be individualized considering gestational age and maternal prognosis. Surgery is generally safe during pregnancy. Systemic therapy may be delayed until postpartum for early-stage. Immune checkpoint inhibitors and targeted therapy are not recommended during pregnancy.
special_situations.evidence: Not specifically addressed in NCCN guideline; general oncology principles apply.

clinical_pearls: Sentinel lymph node biopsy is the most important prognostic tool for clinical stage I/II melanoma; a positive SLN upstages to stage III and is associated with significantly decreased melanoma-specific survival [Montcreif MD, et al. J Clin Oncol 2022;40:3940-3951]. However, SLNB has not been proven to improve RFS or OS, but improves regional nodal control [Crystal JS, et al. JAMA Surg 2022;157:835-842].

For thin melanomas (≤0.75 mm), SLNB is not recommended unless there is uncertainty about microstaging or adverse features. The Merlin_001 trial suggests CP-GEP testing may be used in select T1b ± T2a patients for shared decision-making when the true risk is <10% [Hieken TJ, et al. JAMA Surg 2025;160:1358-1366].

Adjuvant therapy for stage III melanoma should be considered based on risk of recurrence and toxicity. Both anti-PD-1 agents (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) and BRAF/MEK inhibitors (dabrafenib/trametinib) improve relapse-free survival. The impact on overall survival remains undefined for anti-PD-1 agents; COMBI-AD showed a trend but did not cross interim boundary.

For metastatic melanoma, first-line systemic therapy options include combination checkpoint blockade (ipilimumab/nivolumab or nivolumab/relatlimab) or anti-PD-1 monotherapy. BRAF/MEK inhibitors are alternative first-line for BRAF V600-mutant, especially if rapid response needed. DREAMseq trial confirmed superiority of first-line immunotherapy over BRAF/MEK therapy in BRAF-mutant patients [Atkins MB, et al. J Clin Oncol 2023;41:186-197].

The role of completion lymph node dissection after positive SLNB is limited. Two prospective trials (MSLT-II, DeCOG-SLT) showed no benefit in melanoma-specific or overall survival. Nodal basin ultrasound surveillance is preferred over CLND for most patients.

Neoadjuvant immunotherapy (nivolumab/ipilimumab or pembrolizumab) improves event-free survival compared to adjuvant therapy alone in resectable stage III melanoma. NADINA trial: 12-month EFS 84% vs. 57% with neoadjuvant nivolumab/ipilimumab [Blank CU, et al. N Engl J Med 2024;391:1696-1708]. SWOG 1801: 2-year EFS 72% vs. 49% for neoadjuvant pembrolizumab [Patel SP, et al. N Engl J Med 2023;388:813-823].

For patients with brain metastases, nivolumab/ipilimumab combination provides high intracranial response rates (CheckMate 204: 50-57%). BRAF/MEK inhibitors also have intracranial activity (COMBI-MB: 44-58%). Whole brain RT is reserved for palliation when SRS is not feasible.

Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) is an emerging biomarker for minimal residual disease and response assessment, but its clinical utility is not yet defined. Detectable ctDNA after surgery correlates with worse prognosis in adjuvant trials, but integration into clinical decision-making remains unclear [ME-C 3 of 8].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Melanoma: Cutaneous, Version 2.2026
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive guidelines for diagnosis, staging, treatment, and follow-up of cutaneous melanoma. Includes principles of surgery, systemic therapy (adjuvant and metastatic), radiation therapy, biomarker testing, and management of toxicities.

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, Eighth Edition (2017)
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides definitions for T, N, M categories and prognostic stage groups for cutaneous melanoma. Used for staging and prognosis.

guidelines_resources.title: ESMO Clinical Practice Guidelines for Cutaneous Melanoma
guidelines_resources.notes: ESMO provides evidence-based recommendations for diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up.

guidelines_resources.title: SITC Melanoma Immunotherapy Guidelines
guidelines_resources.notes: Focuses on immunotherapy use and management of immune-related adverse events.

guidelines_resources.title: Neoadjuvant Systemic Therapy in Melanoma: Recommendations
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides standardized definitions for pathologic response assessment and recommendations for neoadjuvant trial design [Amaria RN, et al. Lancet Oncol 2019;20:e378-e389].

guidelines_resources.title: Melanoma Treatment (PDQ®) – Health Professional Version
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive, peer-reviewed summary on melanoma treatment.

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Dabrafenib plus Trametinib in Stage III BRAF-Mutated Melanoma
key_trials.population: Resected stage III melanoma with BRAF V600E/K mutation (AJCC 7th ed. IIIA >1 mm, IIIB/C).
key_trials.intervention: Dabrafenib 150 mg BID + trametinib 2 mg QD for 12 months.
key_trials.key_result: 3-year RFS 58% vs. 39% (HR 0.47; 95% CI 0.39-0.58; P<.001). 3-year OS 86% vs. 77% (HR 0.57; 95% CI 0.42-0.79; P=.0006, did not cross interim boundary).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Distant metastasis-free survival (HR 0.51; 95% CI 0.40-0.65; nominal P<.001). Grade 3-4 AEs 41% vs. 14%.
key_trials.practice_change: Established adjuvant BRAF/MEK inhibition as standard for resected stage III BRAF-mutant melanoma. FDA approved.

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Nivolumab versus Ipilimumab in Resected Stage III or IV Melanoma
key_trials.population: Resected stage IIIB/C or stage IV melanoma (AJCC 7th ed.).
key_trials.comparator: Ipilimumab 10 mg/kg Q3W x 4 then Q12W
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: DMFS at 1 year: 80% vs. 73% (HR 0.73; 95% CI 0.55-0.95). OS data not mature at initial report.
key_trials.practice_change: Nivolumab became preferred adjuvant option due to better safety and efficacy than ipilimumab. FDA approved for resected nodal or metastatic melanoma.

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Pembrolizumab versus Placebo in Resected Stage III Melanoma
key_trials.population: Resected stage III melanoma (AJCC 7th ed. IIIA >1 mm, IIIB/C, excluding in-transit).
key_trials.intervention: Pembrolizumab 200 mg Q3W for up to 1 year
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Distant metastasis as first recurrence: HR 0.53; 95% CI 0.37-0.76. OS data not mature.
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab as adjuvant therapy for resected stage III melanoma. FDA approved for lymph node involvement.

key_trials.full_name: Combined Nivolumab and Ipilimumab or Monotherapy in Untreated Melanoma
key_trials.population: Previously untreated unresectable stage III or stage IV melanoma.
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg Q3W x 4, then nivolumab monotherapy
key_trials.comparator: Nivolumab 3 mg/kg Q2W or Ipilimumab 3 mg/kg Q3W x 4
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) for nivolumab ± ipilimumab vs. ipilimumab.
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: combination 11.5 mo, nivolumab 6.9 mo, ipilimumab 2.9 mo. Median OS: combination not reached, nivolumab 36.9 mo, ipilimumab 19.9 mo. 4-year OS: 53%, 46%, 30%.
key_trials.practice_change: Established nivolumab/ipilimumab combination as preferred first-line for many patients due to high response and long-term survival. Nivolumab monotherapy is an alternative for those with contraindications to combination.

key_trials.full_name: Combination Dabrafenib and Trametinib versus Combination Nivolumab and Ipilimumab for Advanced BRAF-Mutant Melanoma
key_trials.population: Previously untreated unresectable or metastatic BRAF V600-mutant melanoma.
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab/ipilimumab followed by dabrafenib/trametinib at progression (arm A) or reverse sequence (arm B).
key_trials.key_result: 2-year OS: arm A 72% vs. arm B 52% (HR 0.53; 95% CI 0.35-0.81).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PFS favored arm A. ORR: combination immunotherapy 46% vs. BRAF/MEK 43%.
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated superiority of first-line immunotherapy over BRAF/MEK targeted therapy in BRAF-mutant metastatic melanoma. Supports current recommendation for immunotherapy as preferred first-line.

key_trials.full_name: Neoadjuvant Nivolumab and Ipilimumab in Resectable Stage III Melanoma
key_trials.population: Resectable stage III melanoma (clinically positive nodes).
key_trials.intervention: Neoadjuvant ipilimumab 80 mg + nivolumab 240 mg Q3W x 2 cycles, then surgery + response-driven adjuvant therapy.
key_trials.key_result: 12-month EFS 83.7% vs. 57.2% (P<.01). MPR 59% in neoadjuvant arm.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS data not mature. Grade 3-4 AEs comparable.
key_trials.practice_change: Established neoadjuvant nivolumab/ipilimumab as preferred approach for resectable stage III disease. Category 1 for initial presentation with clinical nodes.

key_trials.full_name: Neoadjuvant-adjuvant or adjuvant-only pembrolizumab in advanced melanoma
key_trials.intervention: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab 200 mg Q3W x 3 doses, then surgery, then adjuvant pembrolizumab to complete 1 year.
key_trials.practice_change: Established neoadjuvant pembrolizumab as an option for resectable stage III/IV melanoma.

key_trials.full_name: Combined Nivolumab and Ipilimumab in Patients with Melanoma and Active Brain Metastases
key_trials.population: Melanoma with at least one untreated brain metastasis, no prior local therapy for that lesion, asymptomatic.
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg Q3W x 4, then nivolumab monotherapy.
key_trials.key_result: Intracranial response rate 50% (complete response 16%). Grade 3-4 AEs 55%.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median PFS not reached. OS not reached.
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated high intracranial activity of combination immunotherapy. Patients with asymptomatic brain metastases may be treated with upfront systemic therapy.

key_trials.full_name: Dabrafenib plus trametinib in patients with BRAF V600-mutant melanoma brain metastases
key_trials.population: BRAF V600-mutant melanoma with brain metastases (cohort A: asymptomatic, no prior local therapy; cohort B: no prior local therapy, any symptoms; cohort C: prior local therapy, asymptomatic; cohort D: prior local therapy, symptomatic).
key_trials.intervention: Dabrafenib 150 mg BID + trametinib 2 mg QD.
key_trials.key_result: Intracranial response: cohort A 58%, cohort B 56%, cohort C 44%, cohort D 59%. Median OS: 10.8, 24.3, 10.2, 11.5 months respectively.
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated intracranial activity of BRAF/MEK inhibition in patients with brain metastases.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab versus Ipilimumab in Advanced Melanoma
key_trials.population: Previously untreated or one prior therapy (no prior immune checkpoint inhibitor) advanced melanoma.
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: pembro (pooled) not reached vs. ipi 16 mo; 2-year OS 55% vs. 43%. PFS improved (HR ~0.57).
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab as superior to ipilimumab in first-line advanced melanoma. Led to FDA approval.
